› Look at Carpathi's LLM wiki. (https://gist.github.com/karpathy/442a6bf555914893e9891c11519de94f) Then look at this
  repo with all of the functionality for posting lists online and allowing agents to seamlessly update their lists,
  having the lists be basically Git repos so that they can be updated and match the version history and the version
  history tract. as well as The logging in functionality, the database functionality. ZThe API docs, the ability to
  browse using a folder structure. ZThe backbone of the website and the backend, the connection to the backend. I
  want to make a version of this website, but I want it to be able to host LLM wikis, so people can come on there and
  either put their LLM wiki onto this website, or they could take some kind of description of a wiki that they want
  to generate, or some other unstructured data, maybe a wiki in another format, something like that, come on here,
  dump it all in a text input or a file upload, their own wiki that they can update using agents they can update
  themselves, that they can change the settings of, like the viewing settings on a per‑file basis, as seen in this
  repository, and see other people's updates if they're public, etc. Backbones for something like this could include
  Quartz, which is a public Obsidian hoster, other Obsidian hosters that exist, but we want to add the editing
  capabilities and fine grain Permissions. there is also some research in here in research/ that has good info about
  what exists out there, you should also look at what exists. i want to make the thing, talk with me to come up with
  a good spec and make it

llm-wiki-research.md

1→# LLM Wiki Book on ListHub — Research & Plan
     2→
     3→**Date:** 2026-04-06
     4→**Author:** Claude (autonomous research session)
     5→**Status:** Research complete, plan drafted, no implementation yet
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## TL;DR
    10→
    11→1. **"LLM Wiki" is five overlapping concepts**, not one. Karpathy's April 3, 2026 gist crystallized one sense (personal markdown KB maintained by an LLM). The other four are AI-generated public encyclopedias, agent-readable knowledge, traditional wikis-about-LLMs, and human+AI collaborative wikis.
    12→2. **No "LLM Wiki Book" exists.** No archive, no awesome-list, no Hugging Face dataset that catalogs LLM wiki communities. The term is days old. First-mover window is open and measured in **weeks**.
    13→3. **No Moltbook-style preemptive archive of LLM wikis exists.** Closest analogue is `ronantakizawa/moltbook` on Hugging Face. The `{topic}book` naming pattern is a one-off — recommend NOT using "llmwikibook" as a name (reads derivative).
    14→4. **ListHub is structurally a perfect substrate.** Git-backed per-user markdown wikis + REST API + llms.txt = it already IS an LLM wiki platform (senses 1, 3, 5). A directory hosted on ListHub dogfoods the thing it catalogs.
    15→5. **Recommended action:** Build the directory on ListHub, lead with the five-sense taxonomy, ship within 2 weeks.
    16→
    17→---
    18→
    19→## Part 1 — What is an "LLM Wiki"?
    20→
    21→The term is **not a single thing**. As of early April 2026 it umbrellas five distinct meanings, which blog posts and tweets routinely conflate:
    22→
    23→| # | Sense | Who writes it | Who reads it | Example |
    24→|---|---|---|---|---|
    25→| 1 | **Personal LLM wiki (Karpathy sense)** | LLM, on demand | The user | Karpathy's gist |
    26→| 2 | **AI-generated public encyclopedia** | LLM, at scale | The public | Grokipedia, Endless Wiki |
    27→| 3 | **Wiki FOR LLMs to consume** | Humans (mostly) | Other LLMs | llms.txt sites, DeepWiki |
    28→| 4 | **Wiki ABOUT LLMs** | Humans | Humans | Wikipedia "Large language model" |
    29→| 5 | **Human + AI collaborative wiki** | Both | Both | Semiont, AgentWiki |
    30→
    31→The April 2026 Karpathy post (sense 1) is currently capturing the term. A directory project should pick which senses it covers and say so explicitly. The five-sense taxonomy is itself a contribution — most blog posts on the topic don't draw the distinction.
    32→
    33→---
    34→
    35→## Part 2 — Concrete examples (with URLs)
    36→
    37→### Sense 1 — Karpathy-style personal LLM wikis (the new hotness)
    38→- **Karpathy's gist** — https://gist.github.com/karpathy/442a6bf555914893e9891c11519de94f — three-layer architecture (raw / wiki / schema), markdown-first, ~100 articles / 400k words. The canonical reference, posted 2026-04-03.
    39→- **Astro-Han/karpathy-llm-wiki** — https://github.com/Astro-Han/karpathy-llm-wiki — packaged "skill" to build your own.
    40→- **knowledge-engine (tashisleepy)** — https://github.com/tashisleepy/knowledge-engine — Karpathy pattern + Memvid, dual human/machine layer.
    41→- **kfchou/wiki-skills** — https://github.com/kfchou/wiki-skills
    42→- **hellohejinyu/llm-wiki** — https://github.com/hellohejinyu/llm-wiki
    43→- **llmwiki.app** — https://llmwiki.app/
    44→
    45→### Sense 2 — AI-generated public encyclopedias
    46→- **Grokipedia** — https://grokipedia.com/ — xAI, 885k articles, launched Oct 2025. Bias and hallucination criticism.
    47→- **Endless Wiki** — https://www.endlesswiki.com/ — Kimi K2 via Groq, 62k+ pages, on-demand generation.
    48→- **STORM (Stanford)** — https://github.com/stanford-oval/storm — research system for automated Wikipedia article generation.
    49→- **LLMpedia** — https://arxiv.org/html/2603.24080 — academic framework for materializing LLM parametric knowledge.
    50→
    51→### Sense 3 — Wikis FOR LLMs
    52→- **llms.txt ecosystem** — https://llmstxt.org/ — adopted by 780+ companies (Anthropic, Cursor, Cloudflare, Vercel).
    53→- **DeepWiki (Cognition)** — https://deepwiki.com/ — 50,000+ AI-generated repo wikis. URL trick: `github.com` → `deepwiki.com`.
    54→
    55→### Sense 5 — Human + AI collaborative
    56→- **Semiont (AI Alliance)** — https://github.com/The-AI-Alliance/semiont — graph-based, W3C Web Annotation, MCP-exposed.
    57→- **AgentWiki** — https://agentwiki.org/ — DokuWiki + JSON-RPC for agent read/write.
    58→
    59→### Sense 4 — Wikis ABOUT LLMs (for completeness)
    60→- Wikipedia: Large language model, List of large language models
    61→- Wikiversity: Large language models course
    62→- (Note: Wikipedia banned LLM-generated article content in March 2026 — the traditional and LLM-wiki worlds are diverging right now.)
    63→
    64→---
    65→
    66→## Part 3 — Existing directories / meta-resources
    67→
    68→Every directory I could find covers exactly **one** sense, mostly sense 3:
    69→
    70→- **llms-txt-hub** — https://llmstxthub.com/ (David Dias, PR-based, 500+ entries — most credible)
    71→- **llmtxt.app** — https://llmtxt.app/ (~13,000 entries, crawl-based — largest)
    72→- **directory.llmstxt.cloud** — ~1,000 entries, Tally form submissions
    73→- **llms-txt.io/directory** — 90+ companies, curated by category
    74→- **llmstxt.site**, **llmsdirectory.com**, **llmstxtdirectory.org** — smaller variants
    75→- **DeepWiki Directory** — https://deepwiki.directory/ (sense 2 narrowly, repo wikis only)
    76→- **SecretiveShell/Awesome-llms-txt** — GitHub awesome-list
    77→
    78→**Nothing spans all five senses.** Nothing catalogues Karpathy-style personal wikis (the trend is 3 days old). Nothing catalogues sense-5 collaborative wikis as a category.
    79→
    80→---
    81→
    82→## Part 4 — Has anyone built an "LLM Wiki Book"?
    83→
    84→**Direct answer: No.** Searches for "LLM Wiki Book", "LLMWikiBook", "llmwikibook" return nothing. There is no Moltbook-style preemptive archive of the LLM wiki ecosystem, no Archive Team effort, no Hugging Face dataset.
    85→
    86→### Why nothing exists yet
    87→- The Karpathy post is **3 days old** (2026-04-03). There is not yet anything to shut down.
    88→- Archive Team is not engaged with AI-native content preservation as of 2026.
    89→- The traditional wiki world and the LLM wiki world only began diverging in March 2026 (Wikipedia LLM ban).
    90→
    91→### Closest analogue
    92→- **ronantakizawa/moltbook** on Hugging Face — community-collected dataset of Moltbook posts. This is the format template to copy: snapshot the corpus + publish as a HF dataset for permanence.
    93→
    94→### About the `{topic}book` naming pattern
    95→- "Moltbook" is a one-off. Etymology: Clawd → Moltbot → Moltbook (lobster molting + Facebook). No other AI archive uses the `*book` suffix.
    96→- **Recommendation: do NOT name this "llmwikibook".** It reads derivative, signals nothing functional. Better names: `awesome-llm-wikis`, `llmwiki-archive`, `wikiark`, `llm-wiki-index`, or simply hosting it as a ListHub item titled "LLM Wikis".
    97→
    98→---
    99→
   100→## Part 5 — Adjacent landscape: agent-friendly app directories
   101→
   102→Relevant because the LLM Wiki Book is one instance of a broader pattern ListHub should occupy.
   103→
   104→- **MCP server directories** are the most active scene: mcp.so, Smithery, mcpservers.org (claims 20k), ToolSDK (~4.5k), GitHub MCP Registry (~44, official but tiny), plus 5+ awesome-mcp-servers GitHub lists.
   105→- **Agent-API directories** are thin: agentsapis.com is the only real one.
   106→- **PKM directories** (awesome-pkm, awesome-note-taking) are mature but completely agent-unaware — no entry tagged "has llms.txt / has MCP server / has REST API".
   107→- **Has ListHub itself been catalogued?** No. Zero hits. Note: name collides with Move, Inc.'s real-estate MLS service — branding risk worth noting.
   108→
   109→**Biggest unfilled category:** A cross-protocol directory that indexes "has llms.txt AND MCP server AND public REST API". Whoever builds it defines the category. ListHub is structurally suited to host it because it IS a list publishing platform.
   110→
   111→---
   112→
   113→## Part 6 — Plan: LLM Wiki Book on ListHub
   114→
   115→### Should this live on ListHub? — **Yes.**
   116→
   117→Reasons:
   118→1. **Dogfooding.** ListHub is a markdown-list publishing platform with git backing and llms.txt. The directory of LLM wikis is itself an LLM wiki (sense 1 + 3). Hosting it elsewhere would be a missed proof point.
   119→2. **Distribution.** ListHub already has `/@jacobreal/agent-friendly-apps`. The new list slots into the same surface and benefits from the existing agent-first-web positioning (mockups/agentfirst-standard.html, beads ticket listhub-7aw).
   120→3. **Permanence story.** Git-backed → `git clone`-able → can be mirrored to GitHub and Hugging Face later for archive permanence (the Moltbook angle).
   121→
   122→### Structure
   123→
   124→A single ListHub list with the **five-sense taxonomy as section headers**, not a flat collection. Each entry has:
   125→
   126→```yaml
   127→title: <name>
   128→url: <primary URL>
   129→sense: 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5    # which kind of LLM wiki
   130→type: implementation | spec | example | directory | research
   131→maintainer: <person/org>
   132→first_seen: <date>
   133→status: live | archived | research
   134→description: <1-2 sentences>
   135→notes: <optional commentary>
   136→```
   137→
   138→The "sense" field is the key contribution — it forces the taxonomy on the reader.
   139→
   140→### Relationship to existing work
   141→
   142→- **`/@jacobreal/agent-friendly-apps`** — that list catalogs *apps that work well with agents*. The LLM Wiki list catalogs *wikis in the LLM ecosystem*. Different scope. Cross-link both ways.
   143→- **`listhub-7aw.2`** (llms-txt.io directory bi-directional link, 788+ sites) — overlaps sense 3 only. Reference the integration but don't duplicate; the LLM Wiki list should NOT try to be a 13,000-entry llms.txt directory. It should be a ~50-entry curated index across all five senses.
   144→- **`listhub-bd1`** (publishable curated directory UI like flowtools.directory) — the LLM Wiki Book is a natural first customer for that template once it ships.
   145→
   146→### What "aspects public" means here
   147→
   148→ListHub has visibility controls (public/unlisted/private). For the LLM Wiki Book, **everything public**. The whole point is discoverability. No private fields. The list itself is the artifact.
   149→
   150→### Single big list vs community directory?
   151→
   152→**Start as a single curated list, opt into community contributions later.** Reasons:
   153→- The first-mover window is weeks, not months. Solo publishing is faster than coordinating.
   154→- Curation quality matters more than breadth in the early days — 30 hand-picked entries with the taxonomy is more valuable than 300 auto-crawled URLs.
   155→- ListHub already supports `comments / suggested additions` (listhub-zyc), so the path to community contributions exists when ready.
   156→
   157→### Moltbook-style "archive before disappearance" angle
   158→
   159→Apply selectively, not as the framing. The Karpathy ecosystem is too young to be at risk of disappearing yet. But:
   160→- **Snapshot Karpathy's gist content** into the list as a preserved copy (gists can be deleted).
   161→- **Mirror each external wiki's homepage** via the Internet Archive's Wayback Machine and link to the snapshot alongside the live URL.
   162→- **Publish a Hugging Face dataset companion** later — `jacobcole/llm-wikis` — which is the actual `{topic}book`-shaped artifact. Use the `ronantakizawa/moltbook` format as template.
   163→
   164→This gives a graceful "we are archiving this" story without hyperbole, and creates two independent persistence layers (ListHub + HF).
   165→
   166→### Naming
   167→
   168→- **List title on ListHub:** "LLM Wikis" (plural, function-signalling, no `book` suffix).
   169→- **Slug:** `/@jacobreal/llm-wikis`
   170→- **HF dataset (later):** `jacobcole/llm-wikis`
   171→- **Avoid:** llmwikibook, llm-wiki-book — reads derivative.
   172→
   173→---
   174→
   175→## Part 7 — Concrete next steps
   176→
   177→1. **Create the list on ListHub** (this session — API call below)
   178→2. **Seed with ~25 entries** across the five senses, drawing from Part 2 above
   179→3. **Write a top-of-list explainer** containing the five-sense taxonomy
   180→4. **Submit to relevant directories**: Hacker News (Show HN), r/LocalLLaMA, llms-txt-hub PR, post to Karpathy's gist comments
   181→5. **Add Wayback Machine snapshots** for the ~10 most-likely-to-rot entries
   182→6. **(Stretch) Mirror to Hugging Face dataset** for permanence — the `{topic}book` artifact
   183→
   184→### Risks and mitigations
   185→
   186→| Risk | Mitigation |
   187→|---|---|
   188→| Term "LLM wiki" gets captured by Karpathy sense within a month, taxonomy looks pedantic | Title with plural ("LLM Wikis"), explicitly frame as covering a family |
   189→| ListHub name collision with real-estate product hurts discoverability | Submit as "ListHub (globalbr.ai)" everywhere; consider rename later |
   190→| Curation work doesn't scale | Open community contributions via existing comments system once seeded |
   191→| First-mover window closes | Ship within 2 weeks. Don't perfect — publish and iterate. |
   192→

   199→- VentureBeat coverage — https://venturebeat.com/data/karpathy-shares-llm-knowledge-base-architecture-that-bypasses-rag-with-an

**Date:** 2026-04-06
**Author:** Claude (autonomous research session)
**Status:** Research complete, plan drafted, no implementation yet

---

## TL;DR

1. **"LLM Wiki" is five overlapping concepts**, not one. Karpathy's April 3, 2026 gist crystallized one sense (personal markdown KB maintained by an LLM). The other four are AI-generated public encyclopedias, agent-readable knowledge, traditional wikis-about-LLMs, and human+AI collaborative wikis.
2. **No "LLM Wiki Book" exists.** No archive, no awesome-list, no Hugging Face dataset that catalogs LLM wiki communities. The term is days old. First-mover window is open and measured in **weeks**.
3. **No Moltbook-style preemptive archive of LLM wikis exists.** Closest analogue is `ronantakizawa/moltbook` on Hugging Face. The `{topic}book` naming pattern is a one-off — recommend NOT using "llmwikibook" as a name (reads derivative).
4. **ListHub is structurally a perfect substrate.** Git-backed per-user markdown wikis + REST API + llms.txt = it already IS an LLM wiki platform (senses 1, 3, 5). A directory hosted on ListHub dogfoods the thing it catalogs.
5. **Recommended action:** Build the directory on ListHub, lead with the five-sense taxonomy, ship within 2 weeks.

---

## Part 1 — What is an "LLM Wiki"?

The term is **not a single thing**. As of early April 2026 it umbrellas five distinct meanings, which blog posts and tweets routinely conflate:

| # | Sense | Who writes it | Who reads it | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Personal LLM wiki (Karpathy sense)** | LLM, on demand | The user | Karpathy's gist |
| 2 | **AI-generated public encyclopedia** | LLM, at scale | The public | Grokipedia, Endless Wiki |
| 3 | **Wiki FOR LLMs to consume** | Humans (mostly) | Other LLMs | llms.txt sites, DeepWiki |
| 4 | **Wiki ABOUT LLMs** | Humans | Humans | Wikipedia "Large language model" |
| 5 | **Human + AI collaborative wiki** | Both | Both | Semiont, AgentWiki |

The April 2026 Karpathy post (sense 1) is currently capturing the term. A directory project should pick which senses it covers and say so explicitly. The five-sense taxonomy is itself a contribution — most blog posts on the topic don't draw the distinction.

---

## Part 2 — Concrete examples (with URLs)

### Sense 1 — Karpathy-style personal LLM wikis (the new hotness)
- **Karpathy's gist** — https://gist.github.com/karpathy/442a6bf555914893e9891c11519de94f — three-layer architecture (raw / wiki / schema), markdown-first, ~100 articles / 400k words. The canonical reference, posted 2026-04-03.
- **Astro-Han/karpathy-llm-wiki** — https://github.com/Astro-Han/karpathy-llm-wiki — packaged "skill" to build your own.
- **knowledge-engine (tashisleepy)** — https://github.com/tashisleepy/knowledge-engine — Karpathy pattern + Memvid, dual human/machine layer.
- **kfchou/wiki-skills** — https://github.com/kfchou/wiki-skills
- **hellohejinyu/llm-wiki** — https://github.com/hellohejinyu/llm-wiki
- **llmwiki.app** — https://llmwiki.app/

### Sense 2 — AI-generated public encyclopedias
- **Grokipedia** — https://grokipedia.com/ — xAI, 885k articles, launched Oct 2025. Bias and hallucination criticism.
- **Endless Wiki** — https://www.endlesswiki.com/ — Kimi K2 via Groq, 62k+ pages, on-demand generation.
- **STORM (Stanford)** — https://github.com/stanford-oval/storm — research system for automated Wikipedia article generation.
- **LLMpedia** — https://arxiv.org/html/2603.24080 — academic framework for materializing LLM parametric knowledge.

### Sense 3 — Wikis FOR LLMs
- **llms.txt ecosystem** — https://llmstxt.org/ — adopted by 780+ companies (Anthropic, Cursor, Cloudflare, Vercel).
- **DeepWiki (Cognition)** — https://deepwiki.com/ — 50,000+ AI-generated repo wikis. URL trick: `github.com` → `deepwiki.com`.

### Sense 5 — Human + AI collaborative
- **Semiont (AI Alliance)** — https://github.com/The-AI-Alliance/semiont — graph-based, W3C Web Annotation, MCP-exposed.
- **AgentWiki** — https://agentwiki.org/ — DokuWiki + JSON-RPC for agent read/write.

### Sense 4 — Wikis ABOUT LLMs (for completeness)
- Wikipedia: Large language model, List of large language models
- Wikiversity: Large language models course
- (Note: Wikipedia banned LLM-generated article content in March 2026 — the traditional and LLM-wiki worlds are diverging right now.)

---

## Part 3 — Existing directories / meta-resources

Every directory I could find covers exactly **one** sense, mostly sense 3:

- **llms-txt-hub** — https://llmstxthub.com/ (David Dias, PR-based, 500+ entries — most credible)
- **llmtxt.app** — https://llmtxt.app/ (~13,000 entries, crawl-based — largest)
- **directory.llmstxt.cloud** — ~1,000 entries, Tally form submissions
- **llms-txt.io/directory** — 90+ companies, curated by category
- **llmstxt.site**, **llmsdirectory.com**, **llmstxtdirectory.org** — smaller variants
- **DeepWiki Directory** — https://deepwiki.directory/ (sense 2 narrowly, repo wikis only)
- **SecretiveShell/Awesome-llms-txt** — GitHub awesome-list

**Nothing spans all five senses.** Nothing catalogues Karpathy-style personal wikis (the trend is 3 days old). Nothing catalogues sense-5 collaborative wikis as a category.

---

## Part 4 — Has anyone built an "LLM Wiki Book"?

**Direct answer: No.** Searches for "LLM Wiki Book", "LLMWikiBook", "llmwikibook" return nothing. There is no Moltbook-style preemptive archive of the LLM wiki ecosystem, no Archive Team effort, no Hugging Face dataset.

### Why nothing exists yet
- The Karpathy post is **3 days old** (2026-04-03). There is not yet anything to shut down.
- Archive Team is not engaged with AI-native content preservation as of 2026.
- The traditional wiki world and the LLM wiki world only began diverging in March 2026 (Wikipedia LLM ban).

### Closest analogue

### About the `{topic}book` naming pattern
- "Moltbook" is a one-off. Etymology: Clawd → Moltbot → Moltbook (lobster molting + Facebook). No other AI archive uses the `*book` suffix.
- **Recommendation: do NOT name this "llmwikibook".** It reads derivative, signals nothing functional. Better names: `awesome-llm-wikis`, `llmwiki-archive`, `wikiark`, `llm-wiki-index`, or simply hosting it as a ListHub item titled "LLM Wikis".

---

## Part 5 — Adjacent landscape: agent-friendly app directories

Relevant because the LLM Wiki Book is one instance of a broader pattern ListHub should occupy.

- **MCP server directories** are the most active scene: mcp.so, Smithery, mcpservers.org (claims 20k), ToolSDK (~4.5k), GitHub MCP Registry (~44, official but tiny), plus 5+ awesome-mcp-servers GitHub lists.
- **Agent-API directories** are thin: agentsapis.com is the only real one.
- **PKM directories** (awesome-pkm, awesome-note-taking) are mature but completely agent-unaware — no entry tagged "has llms.txt / has MCP server / has REST API".
- **Has ListHub itself been catalogued?** No. Zero hits. Note: name collides with Move, Inc.'s real-estate MLS service — branding risk worth noting.

**Biggest unfilled category:** A cross-protocol directory that indexes "has llms.txt AND MCP server AND public REST API". Whoever builds it defines the category. ListHub is structurally suited to host it because it IS a list publishing platform.

---

## Part 6 — Plan: LLM Wiki Book on ListHub

### Should this live on ListHub? — **Yes.**

Reasons:
1. **Dogfooding.** ListHub is a markdown-list publishing platform with git backing and llms.txt. The directory of LLM wikis is itself an LLM wiki (sense 1 + 3). Hosting it elsewhere would be a missed proof point.
2. **Distribution.** ListHub already has `/@jacobreal/agent-friendly-apps`. The new list slots into the same surface and benefits from the existing agent-first-web positioning (mockups/agentfirst-standard.html, beads ticket listhub-7aw).
3. **Permanence story.** Git-backed → `git clone`-able → can be mirrored to GitHub and Hugging Face later for archive permanence (the Moltbook angle).

### Structure

```yaml
title: <name>
url: <primary URL>
sense: 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5    # which kind of LLM wiki

maintainer: <person/org>
first_seen: <date>

notes: <optional commentary>
```

The "sense" field is the key contribution — it forces the taxonomy on the reader.

### Relationship to existing work

- **`/@jacobreal/agent-friendly-apps`** — that list catalogs *apps that work well with agents*. The LLM Wiki list catalogs *wikis in the LLM ecosystem*. Different scope. Cross-link both ways.
- **`listhub-7aw.2`** (llms-txt.io directory bi-directional link, 788+ sites) — overlaps sense 3 only. Reference the integration but don't duplicate; the LLM Wiki list should NOT try to be a 13,000-entry llms.txt directory. It should be a ~50-entry curated index across all five senses.
- **`listhub-bd1`** (publishable curated directory UI like flowtools.directory) — the LLM Wiki Book is a natural first customer for that template once it ships.

### What "aspects public" means here

ListHub has visibility controls (public/unlisted/private). For the LLM Wiki Book, **everything public**. The whole point is discoverability. No private fields. The list itself is the artifact.

### Single big list vs community directory?

**Start as a single curated list, opt into community contributions later.** Reasons:
- The first-mover window is weeks, not months. Solo publishing is faster than coordinating.
- Curation quality matters more than breadth in the early days — 30 hand-picked entries with the taxonomy is more valuable than 300 auto-crawled URLs.
- ListHub already supports `comments / suggested additions` (listhub-zyc), so the path to community contributions exists when ready.

### Moltbook-style "archive before disappearance" angle

Apply selectively, not as the framing. The Karpathy ecosystem is too young to be at risk of disappearing yet. But:
- **Snapshot Karpathy's gist content** into the list as a preserved copy (gists can be deleted).
- **Mirror each external wiki's homepage** via the Internet Archive's Wayback Machine and link to the snapshot alongside the live URL.
- **Publish a Hugging Face dataset companion** later — `jacobcole/llm-wikis` — which is the actual `{topic}book`-shaped artifact. Use the `ronantakizawa/moltbook` format as template.

This gives a graceful "we are archiving this" story without hyperbole, and creates two independent persistence layers (ListHub + HF).

### Naming

- **List title on ListHub:** "LLM Wikis" (plural, function-signalling, no `book` suffix).
- **Slug:** `/@jacobreal/llm-wikis`
- **HF dataset (later):** `jacobcole/llm-wikis`
- **Avoid:** llmwikibook, llm-wiki-book — reads derivative.

### Risks and mitigations

| Risk | Mitigation |
|---|---|
| Term "LLM wiki" gets captured by Karpathy sense within a month, taxonomy looks pedantic | Title with plural ("LLM Wikis"), explicitly frame as covering a family |
| ListHub name collision with real-estate product hurts discoverability | Submit as "ListHub (globalbr.ai)" everywhere; consider rename later |
| Curation work doesn't scale | Open community contributions via existing comments system once seeded |
| First-mover window closes | Ship within 2 weeks. Don't perfect — publish and iterate. |

   196→## Observations
   197→
   198→1. **Sense 1 exploded in days.** Karpathy posted April 3, 2026; this catalog found ~20 GitHub implementations and ~12 "I built one" blog posts within 72 hours. The ecosystem is moving faster than any directory can follow.
   199→
   200→2. **Sense 2 has two distinct sub-categories**: general-purpose AI encyclopedias (Grokipedia, Endless Wiki, WikiGen) and repo-docs generators (DeepWiki and ~10 clones). Treating them together understates how different the use cases are.
   201→
   202→3. **Sense 5 is overwhelmingly retrofit.** ~17 of the ~20 sense-5 entries are MCP adapters bolted onto pre-existing wikis. Only 5 projects are designed from scratch as peer surfaces: Semiont, AgentWiki, originlabs/agent-wiki, Ar9av/obsidian-wiki, HiClaw. The frontier is small enough to read all of them.
   203→

# LLM Wikis — Catalog

**Date:** 2026-04-06
**Scope:** Public, accessible wikis in the "LLM wiki" ecosystem across all 5 senses of the term. Companion to `research/llm-wiki-research.md`.
**Total entries:** ~85

The five senses (from the research doc):

1. **Personal LLM wiki (Karpathy sense)** — LLM curates a markdown knowledge base for one user
2. **AI-generated public encyclopedia** — LLM writes articles at scale for a public audience
3. **Wikis FOR LLMs to consume** — docs shaped to be ingested by LLMs (llms.txt, DeepWiki)
4. **Wikis ABOUT LLMs** — traditional human-written reference content about LLMs
5. **Human + AI collaborative wikis** — shared surfaces where humans and agents both read/write

---

## Sense 1 — Personal LLM wikis (Karpathy-style)

### Named personal wikis (the people who actually built one)

Most builders keep their wiki private — same reason as Farza below. This is the short list of real, named, verified instances. The interesting research subject is **the people**, not the tooling.

- **Farzapedia** — Farza Majeed (@FarzaTV), buildspace founder. Tweeted April 4, 2026; boosted by Karpathy same day. ~400 articles, ~2500 source entries. **Explicitly private** — "Considering it made an entire 'Relationship History' article, some things should remain between me and my LLM." Lukewarm landing page: https://farza.com/knowledge — Twitter announcement: https://x.com/FarzaTV/status/2040563939797504467 — Karpathy boost: https://x.com/karpathy/status/2040572272944324650
- **Commonplace** — `zby` (HN: zbyforgotpass) — https://zby.github.io/commonplace/ — A wiki *about* LLM agent memory systems, maintained by LLM agents (recursive). ~30+ system reviews, hundreds of notes. GitHub Pages. The most rigorous publicly-browsable example.
- **Grimoire** — sprites.app dev (HN: 0123456789ABCDE) — https://grimoire-pt5.sprites.app/ — Self-described "LLM-maintained personal knowledge base" following Karpathy. Themes: AI research, infra, security. "Spells/scrolls/grimoire" framing. Active April 2026 commits.
- **Karpathy's own research wiki** — Andrej Karpathy. ~100 articles, ~400k words on an ML research topic. Described in the gist; **not linked publicly**. Private.
- **Louis Wang's KB** — https://louiswang524.github.io/blog/llm-knowledge-base/ — KB itself is private, but the writeup contains the best scaling analysis publicly available ("~100-200 articles is the index-first ceiling"). Maintainer: Louis Wang.

**Pattern observations:**
- "{Name}pedia" is unclaimed as a naming convention — Farzapedia put it on the map but no product owns it.
- `index.md` at root is the universal entry pattern (Farzapedia, Karpathy, Commonplace).
- The unit of value is the navigable filesystem, not the rendered HTML — 3 of 4 verified wikis are agent-first; humans browse second.
- The publishing gap is real: no popular product exists for "publish my LLM-maintained wiki to a public URL with selective visibility." Obsidian Publish isn't LLM-aware; Quartz isn't packaged with an agent loop. Real practitioners (zby, the grimoire owner) are obvious early-adopter targets for a publish-my-LLM-wiki feature.

### Canonical & tooling

- **Karpathy's llm-wiki gist** — https://gist.github.com/karpathy/442a6bf555914893e9891c11519de94f — The original 2026-04-03 "idea file" describing the three-layer raw/wiki/schema pattern. ~8200 stars.
- **llmwiki.app** — https://llmwiki.app/ — Hosted free open-source implementation that builds a compounding wiki via Claude.

### Implementations (GitHub)

- **Astro-Han/karpathy-llm-wiki** — https://github.com/Astro-Han/karpathy-llm-wiki — One Agent Skill for Claude Code / Cursor / Codex.
- **tashisleepy/knowledge-engine** — https://github.com/tashisleepy/knowledge-engine — Karpathy pattern + Memvid; dual human/machine layer.
- **kfchou/wiki-skills** — https://github.com/kfchou/wiki-skills — Claude Code skills (init, ingest, query, lint, update).
- **hellohejinyu/llm-wiki** — https://github.com/hellohejinyu/llm-wiki — Personal wiki CLI with multi-step ReAct query agent.
- **Ar9av/obsidian-wiki** — https://github.com/Ar9av/obsidian-wiki — Markdown skills for building an Obsidian wiki with any agent.
- **Ss1024sS/LLM-wiki** — https://github.com/Ss1024sS/LLM-wiki — Direct port of Karpathy's gist.

- **obsidian-wiki (Ar9av)** — https://github.com/Ar9av/obsidian-wiki — Framework for agents to maintain a shared Obsidian wiki.
- **HiClaw (agentscope-ai)** — https://github.com/agentscope-ai/HiClaw — Collaborative multi-agent OS where humans/agents share state via Matrix.

### MCP-exposed wikis (retrofit)

Local-first / structured-note apps with MCP bridges:
- **Anytype + anytype-mcp** — https://github.com/anyproto/anytype-mcp — Encrypted local-first collaborative wiki; official MCP.
- **Tana (Local API + MCP)** — https://outliner.tana.inc/docs/local-api-mcp — Community MCP: https://github.com/tim-mcdonnell/tana-mcp
- **Notion MCP (official)** — https://developers.notion.com/docs/mcp — Source: https://github.com/makenotion/notion-mcp-server

Traditional wiki engines with MCP bridges:
- **Wiki.js MCP Server** — https://github.com/jaalbin24/wikijs-mcp-server — Search/read/create/update via GraphQL.
- **MediaWiki MCP Server (Professional Wiki)** — https://github.com/ProfessionalWiki/MediaWikiMcpServer — OAuth-gated.
- **BookStack MCP Server** — https://github.com/oculairmedia/Bookstack-MCP — 47-58 endpoints as MCP tools.
- **Confluence MCP (mcp-atlassian)** — https://github.com/sooperset/mcp-atlassian — Read/write/append/archive Confluence Cloud.
- **Azure DevOps Wiki MCP** — https://github.com/uright/azure-devops-wiki-mcp
- **GitHub Wiki MCP** — https://github.com/andreahaku/github_wiki_mcp
- **DokuWiki MCP (doobidoo)** — https://github.com/doobidoo/dokuwiki-mcp-server — Zero-dep, JWT auth.
- **DokuWiki AIAgent plugin** — https://forum.dokuwiki.org/d/25934-plugin-introduction-aiagent-an-ai-agent-for-your-wiki
- **MediaWiki AIEditingAssistant** — https://www.mediawiki.org/wiki/Extension:AIEditingAssistant — VisualEditor-integrated; AI edits in the same history as human edits.
- **SilverBullet MCP** — https://github.com/Ahmad-A0/silverbullet-mcp — Self-hosted Markdown/Lua wiki bridge.

Personal PKM with MCP bridges:
- **Obsidian MCP Server (cyanheads)** — https://github.com/cyanheads/obsidian-mcp-server — Most comprehensive Obsidian MCP.
- **Obsidian MCP (StevenStavrakis)** — https://github.com/StevenStavrakis/obsidian-mcp
- **mcpvault (bitbonsai)** — https://github.com/bitbonsai/mcpvault — Frontmatter-safe Obsidian MCP.
- **Logseq MCP (ergut)** — https://github.com/ergut/mcp-logseq
- **logseq-mcp-tools (joelhooks)** — https://github.com/joelhooks/logseq-mcp-tools

---

## Observations

1. **Sense 1 exploded in days.** Karpathy posted April 3, 2026; this catalog found ~20 GitHub implementations and ~12 "I built one" blog posts within 72 hours. The ecosystem is moving faster than any directory can follow.

2. **Sense 2 has two distinct sub-categories**: general-purpose AI encyclopedias (Grokipedia, Endless Wiki, WikiGen) and repo-docs generators (DeepWiki and ~10 clones). Treating them together understates how different the use cases are.

3. **Sense 5 is overwhelmingly retrofit.** ~17 of the ~20 sense-5 entries are MCP adapters bolted onto pre-existing wikis. Only 5 projects are designed from scratch as peer surfaces: Semiont, AgentWiki, originlabs/agent-wiki, Ar9av/obsidian-wiki, HiClaw. The frontier is small enough to read all of them.

4. **Implication for ListHub:** Shipping an MCP server around the existing REST API is the single highest-leverage move — it adds ListHub to the sense-5 MCP-bridge category instantly (~20 peers), and because ListHub is already git-backed markdown it also qualifies structurally for senses 1 and 3.

5. **Gaps in the ecosystem:** AFFiNE, Roam, Capacities, and Outline have no notable MCP servers — open opportunity. No directory yet includes Karpathy-style personal wikis. No archival project exists.

input.prompt: Summarize what Karpathy means by an "LLM wiki": structure, purpose, sections, how it's used by agents, format (markdown? frontmatter?), and any notable conventions.

content.content:
# Karpathy's LLM Wiki Pattern

## Core Concept

An LLM wiki is a **persistent, compounded knowledge base** that sits between raw source documents and the user. Rather than retrieving fragments at query time (like traditional RAG), the LLM incrementally maintains a structured markdown wiki that synthesizes, cross-references, and updates continuously.

## Structure

The pattern has three layers:

1. **Raw sources** — immutable documents (articles, papers, images) that the LLM reads but never modifies
2. **The wiki** — LLM-generated markdown files containing summaries, entity pages, concept pages, and cross-references
3. **The schema** — configuration document (e.g., CLAUDE.md) specifying wiki conventions, page types, and workflows

## Purpose & Operations

**Ingest**: When you add a source, the LLM reads it, writes summaries, updates relevant pages, and maintains cross-references across 10-15 wiki files simultaneously.

**Query**: Ask questions against the wiki; the LLM synthesizes answers from existing pages. Useful answers become new wiki pages, compounding knowledge over time.

**Lint**: Periodically check for contradictions, orphaned pages, stale claims, and missing cross-references—keeping the wiki healthy as it grows.

## Format & Conventions

- **Markdown files** organized by category (entities, concepts, sources)
- **index.md** — content-oriented catalog of all pages with summaries
- **log.md** — append-only chronological record of ingests, queries, lint passes
- **Frontmatter** — optional YAML metadata for tagging, dating, source counts
- **Wikilinks** — `[[cross-references]]` between pages

## Key Insight

As stated: "The wiki is just a git repo of markdown files. You get version history, branching, and collaboration for free." The human curates sources and asks questions; the LLM handles all maintenance—updating links, revising claims, and keeping structure coherent.

content.text:
`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- ListHub is already ~80% of what you're describing: per-user bare git repos, markdown items with YAML frontmatter, per-item visibility, REST API for agents, FTS search, git push/pull. The missing pieces for "LLM wiki host" are: **ingestion** (dump unstructured → structured wiki), **agent-run loops** (ingest/query/lint as Karpathy describes), **social browse** (other people's public wikis + fork), and **rendering** (wikilinks, index.md as landing).
- Karpathy's pattern is three layers (`raw/` / `wiki/` / `schema.md`). ListHub currently flattens everything into one bag of items — the first real schema decision is whether "a wiki" is a *folder convention* inside a user's existing repo, or a *new first-class object* (`wikis/<slug>/...`) with its own visibility root.
- Your research doc already identifies the unfilled niche: "publish my LLM-maintained wiki to a public URL with selective visibility." Nobody owns this. Quartz/Obsidian Publish aren't LLM-aware; llmwiki.app hosts but doesn't socialize. That's the wedge.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

Before I write a spec, I need to pin down four forks in the road. These determine everything downstream.

questions.question: Should this be built on top of ListHub (same codebase/server) or as a new sibling product?

options.label: Extend ListHub in place (Recommended)
options.description: Add a 'wikis' first-class object to the existing Flask app. Reuses auth, git backend, visibility, API, FTS. Fastest path; dogfoods the research doc's recommendation.

options.label: Fork ListHub into new repo
options.description: Clone the codebase, rebrand, evolve independently. Clean brand separation but you maintain two codebases and lose shared users.

options.label: New app, ListHub as backend via API
options.description: Build a fresh frontend (e.g. Next.js) that talks to ListHub's REST API. Nice separation but adds a network hop and a second deploy.

questions.question: How does a user get a wiki onto the platform? (Pick all that should exist in v1.)

options.label: git push existing wiki
options.description: User already has Karpathy-pattern repo locally; pushes to listhub remote. Zero server-side LLM cost. Matches existing listhub git flow.

options.label: Upload folder / zip of markdown
options.description: Web drag-drop; we unpack into the user's wiki repo. Good for Obsidian vault imports.

options.label: Paste unstructured text → LLM generates wiki
options.description: Big textarea / file upload. Server runs an ingest agent that produces index.md + entity/concept pages. Requires us to run/bill LLM calls.

options.label: Connect URL / existing public wiki
options.description: Point at a github repo or URL and we clone/scrape into a wiki. Cheap for sense-3 mirroring.

questions.question: Where do the ingest/query/lint agents actually run?

options.label: Client-side only — we host, they bring the agent (Recommended for v0)
options.description: We expose MCP + REST; users point Claude Code / Cursor / their own agent at their wiki. No LLM bills for us. Matches current listhub-as-infrastructure philosophy in CLAUDE.md.

options.label: Server-side agent runs too
options.description: We run ingest/query/lint jobs on our server with our own API key (or BYO key). Nicer UX — works from the web UI with no local setup — but we run a job queue and pay for tokens.

options.label: Both: client-first, server as optional convenience
options.description: Ship client-side first; add a 'Run ingest on server (BYO key)' button later. Kicks the job-queue decision down the road.

questions.question: What's the unit of visibility / the first-class object?

options.label: New 'Wiki' object, per-file visibility inside (Recommended)
options.description: A user can have N wikis; each has its own slug, settings, and a tree of pages with public/unlisted/private per file. Clean URL `/@user/wiki-slug/page`. Forkable as a unit.

options.label: Reuse existing items, add a 'wiki' tag + folder convention
options.description: No schema change. A wiki is 'all items in folder X'. Ships in a day but gets confusing as users accumulate multiple wikis and the landing/fork UX is awkward.

options.label: One wiki per user (like Obsidian Publish)

content.text:
Locked in so far:
- **New repo, from scratch.** Copy listhub's architectural DNA (per-user bare git repos, REST API, API keys, per-file visibility) but don't graft onto the existing codebase.
- **v1 ingestion:** git push + folder/zip upload. Tickets for v2: paste-text→LLM, URL/repo connect.
- **Agents: client-side in v1** — but the "point your coding agent at this and say 'make me a wiki'" flow is *the pitch*, so MCP server + REST + API keys must be first-class on day one. Ticket for v2: server-hosted agent with our provided key (sub-decision deferred: headless coding agent vs Anthropic tool-use API).
- **First-class `Wiki` object** with per-file visibility inside.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- The "from scratch" call is the right one even though listhub is 80% there, because a Wiki is structurally *not* a list — it has a **tree** (folders), **wikilinks** (graph), an **index.md root**, and is **forkable as a unit**. Grafting those onto listhub's flat item model would fight the existing schema at every turn. Starting fresh lets the data model be tree-native and fork-native from day one.
- The client-side-agent-first decision is actually a strong move: because the server runs no LLM, the product is a *piece of infrastructure* (like GitHub) rather than an *AI app* (like llmwiki.app). That's the listhub philosophy scaled up — "we're the memory layer, bring your own brain." Pricing and trust stories are both easier.
- One subtle thing to flag: if `Wiki` is forkable and git-backed, you get "GitHub for LLM wikis" almost for free — `fork`, `clone`, `pull request` (suggested edits from agents), `stars`. That's a much bigger product than "Obsidian Publish with agents" and costs ~the same to build if you plan for it now. Worth picking a side before writing code.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

Four more forks and I'll have enough to draft the spec.

questions.question: How ambitious is the social / collaboration layer in v1?

options.label: GitHub-for-wikis (Recommended)
options.description: Public wikis are forkable, cloneable, star-able. Agents can open 'suggested edits' (PR-like) against others' public wikis. Biggest product, but most of it is free from the git backend.

options.label: Obsidian-Publish-with-browse
options.description: Public wikis are read-only to others. You can browse /explore, see others' wikis, but no fork/PR. Simpler UI; loses the collaboration wedge.

options.label: Private-first, public later
options.description: v1 is just hosting + your own agent editing your own wiki. Public browse is a v2 feature. Safest but throws away the first-mover directory opportunity from the research doc.

questions.question: How opinionated is the wiki schema?

options.label: Karpathy three-layer, enforced with gentle nudges (Recommended)
options.description: New wiki gets scaffolded `raw/`, `wiki/`, `schema.md`, `index.md`, `log.md`. Not rigidly enforced — users can ignore it — but templates, agent hints, and the default render assume the pattern. Opinionated default, escape hatch exists.

options.label: Unopinionated — just a tree of markdown
options.description: Any folder structure. No scaffolding. Renders any .md. Maximally flexible; loses the 'this is the canonical LLM-wiki host' positioning.

options.label: Strict — enforce the three-layer at the API level
options.description: API rejects writes that don't fit raw/wiki/schema. Cleanest data, hostile to existing Obsidian/Quartz users who want to migrate.

questions.question: How should wikis render to human readers?

options.label: Server-rendered Jinja like listhub, with wikilink resolver (Recommended)
options.description: Each page is rendered on request from the bare repo. Wikilinks `[[Page]]` resolve. Graph view + index.md as landing. Matches listhub's no-JS-framework convention. Ships fast.

options.label: Quartz-style static site generation per wiki
options.description: On push, regenerate a static site per wiki (Quartz / Astro). Prettier defaults, Obsidian-compatible out of the box, but adds a build step and a storage layer for rendered output.

options.label: Markdown-source only, no HTML render
options.description: Public URL returns raw .md (or rendered minimally). Optimizes for agent/LLM readers, ignores human readers. Radical but on-brand for 'infrastructure for agents'.

questions.question: Stack for the new repo?

options.label: Copy listhub exactly (Flask + SQLite + Jinja + bare git)
options.description: Proven, deployed, the git-smart-HTTP + hook + plumbing code is already debugged. You can literally copy git_backend.py and git_sync.py. Fastest path. Boring is good.

options.label: Flask + Postgres + Jinja + bare git
options.description: Same stack, swap SQLite for Postgres for the social/fork features (many-to-many, concurrent writes to shared tables). Small upgrade, buys you room to grow.

options.label: Next.js + Postgres + bare git (Node git-http-backend)
options.description: Modern frontend, SSR, nicer DX for graph views and fork UIs. Rewrites the git plumbing in Node. Biggest jump; longest road to v1.

content.text:
Good — everything I need. Here's the draft spec. I'll propose 3 names at the end; pick one and I'll start on the scaffold.

---

# Spec — LLM Wiki Host (codename TBD)

## One-liner
**GitHub for LLM wikis.** A hosting platform where humans and their coding agents collaboratively build, version, publish, and fork Karpathy-style markdown knowledge bases, with per-file visibility and a native agent API.

## Positioning
- *Not* llmwiki.app (which runs the agent for you).
- *Not* Obsidian Publish (no agent story, no fork).
- *Not* Quartz (static, solo, no collaboration).
- *Is* the memory layer + social graph + git host for the Karpathy pattern. We host; your agent writes.

## Core principles (inherited from listhub CLAUDE.md)
- **Infrastructure, not app.** The platform stands alone. Agents are clients.
- **Separate repos, seamless integration.** A coding agent should need <50 lines to fully operate a wiki here (bootstrap token, read/write files, search, set visibility, fork).
- **API for writes, git pull for reads.** Same split as listhub — avoids merge conflicts.
- **YAGNI.** Nothing ships in v1 that isn't on the critical path to "my coding agent just built me a public LLM wiki, someone else forked it, and their agent improved it."

## Data model

```
User
  id, username, display_name, email, password_hash, noos_id
  api_keys: [ApiKey]
  wikis: [Wiki]

Wiki                          # the first-class object
  id (nanoid)
  owner_id → User
  slug                        # /@user/<slug>
  title, description
  visibility                  # public | unlisted | private (wiki-level default)
  forked_from_id → Wiki?      # null for originals
  star_count
  created_at, updated_at
  bare_repo_path              # repos/<user>/<slug>.git

Page                          # one row per .md file in the wiki
  id
  wiki_id → Wiki
  path                        # e.g. "wiki/entities/karpathy.md"
  title                       # from frontmatter or filename
  visibility                  # public | unlisted | private (overrides wiki default)
  frontmatter (json)
  content_excerpt             # for search
  updated_at

Wikilink                      # derived, for graph/backlinks
  from_page_id, to_path, resolved_page_id?

Star, Fork, SuggestedEdit     # social
```

Per-file visibility works exactly like listhub: a page marked `private` inside a `public` wiki is hidden from anonymous viewers, API returns 404 without auth, git clone returns the file only if the cloner's key belongs to the owner.

## Storage layout

```
repos/<username>/<wiki-slug>.git        # bare repo, one per wiki
  refs/, objects/, HEAD, hooks/post-receive

Working structure inside each wiki (scaffolded on create):
  schema.md        # conventions for this wiki (agent reads on every session)
  index.md         # landing page — catalog of all pages
  log.md           # append-only ingest/query/lint log
  raw/             # immutable source material
  wiki/            # LLM-curated pages
    entities/
    concepts/
    sources/
```

New wikis are seeded with these files + a `.listhub-wiki` marker file (version-bumpable). **Gentle nudge, not enforced** — users can ignore the structure; renderer handles any tree.

## Rendering
- Server-rendered Jinja, same convention as listhub.
- `[[Wikilink]]` resolver: scans pages on write, stores `Wikilink` rows, resolves by title or path.
- `/@user/<wiki>` renders `index.md` as landing.
- `/@user/<wiki>/<path>` renders any page.
- `/@user/<wiki>/graph` renders a backlink graph (v1: simple HTML list of backlinks per page; D3 force-graph = v2).
- `/@user/<wiki>/log` renders `log.md`.
- Raw markdown available at `?format=md` and `/raw/<path>` (for LLM consumers — this is the llms.txt-style surface).

## Agent surface (the core pitch)

**Every wiki is addressable by a coding agent with a single config block:**

```bash
export WIKIHOST_URL=https://wikihost.example/
export WIKIHOST_TOKEN=<api-key>
export WIKIHOST_WIKI=@me/my-wiki
```

Then git push/pull *or* REST. REST endpoints (mirrors listhub patterns):

```
POST   /api/v1/wikis                           # create a wiki
GET    /api/v1/wikis/:owner/:slug
PATCH  /api/v1/wikis/:owner/:slug              # title, description, visibility
DELETE /api/v1/wikis/:owner/:slug
POST   /api/v1/wikis/:owner/:slug/fork         # returns new wiki under caller

GET    /api/v1/wikis/:owner/:slug/pages                # list (filtered by visibility)
GET    /api/v1/wikis/:owner/:slug/pages/*path          # read page (md + frontmatter)
PUT    /api/v1/wikis/:owner/:slug/pages/*path          # write/create page
PATCH  /api/v1/wikis/:owner/:slug/pages/*path          # update frontmatter/visibility
DELETE /api/v1/wikis/:owner/:slug/pages/*path

GET    /api/v1/wikis/:owner/:slug/search?q=...         # FTS5 across pages
GET    /api/v1/wikis/:owner/:slug/graph                # wikilinks graph JSON
POST   /api/v1/wikis/:owner/:slug/log                  # append to log.md

POST   /api/v1/wikis/:owner/:slug/suggested-edits      # PR-like (for forks)
```

All writes update the bare repo via the same plumbing path listhub uses (`GIT_INDEX_FILE`, `hash-object`, `update-index --cacheinfo`, `write-tree`, `commit-tree`, `update-ref`). `post-receive` hook parses pushes and syncs back to the DB — same two-way pattern as listhub.

**MCP server**: thin wrapper over the REST API. One binary, `wikihost-mcp`, exposing the above as MCP tools. This is what makes "just point your agent at it" actually work out of the box.

## Social layer (v1 scope)

- `/explore` — list of recently updated public wikis, by stars, by tag.
- `/@user` — user profile with their public wikis.
- **Fork** — `POST .../fork` does a server-side git clone of the bare repo under the caller's namespace, copies Page rows, sets `forked_from_id`. Free from the bare-repo model.
- **Star** — one row, one counter.
- **Suggested edits** — a suggested edit from a fork is stored as `{source_wiki, source_commit, target_wiki, diff}`. v1 UI: list + accept/reject (accept = cherry-pick commit into target's bare repo, triggering post-receive). No inline review UI in v1.
- **Comments / discussion** — **v2 ticket.**

## Auth
- Copy listhub: bcrypt passwords, SHA-256 API keys, Flask-Login sessions.
- **Noos OAuth SSO from day one** (users already have Noos accounts — zero-friction signup).
- Git Smart HTTP Basic Auth accepts password *or* API key.
- Admin token for hook calls.

## Ingestion (v1)
1. **`git push`** — user has a wiki repo locally, adds our remote, pushes. `post-receive` scans for `.md`, populates Page rows, resolves wikilinks.
2. **Zip/folder upload** — web UI drag-drop. Server unpacks into a temp dir, commits to the bare repo with message "Initial import from upload". Same post-receive path runs.
3. **Scaffold a blank wiki** — "Create wiki" button seeds the three-layer skeleton with one initial commit. Then the user points their agent at it.

## Stack
- **Flask + Postgres + Jinja + bare git** (chosen).
- Port `git_backend.py`, `git_sync.py`, `hooks/post-receive` from listhub — the plumbing-on-bare-repos code is debugged and worth copying verbatim, just generalize paths for multi-wiki.
- Postgres instead of SQLite because: concurrent writes on shared tables (stars, forks, suggested edits), JSONB for frontmatter, `tsvector` for search (replaces FTS5).
- Gunicorn + nginx + Cloudflare, same as listhub.
- Deploy: fresh Lightsail instance (or the same box under a different systemd unit + nginx vhost — decide at deploy time).

## v1 milestones (in order)

1. **Repo + stack skeleton** — Flask app factory, Postgres schema, Alembic migrations, templates dir, CI.
2. **Auth** — signup/login, API keys, Noos OAuth, admin token.
3. **Wiki CRUD + bare repo provisioning** — create wiki scaffolds the three-layer tree with an initial commit.
4. **Git Smart HTTP + post-receive sync** — port from listhub, generalize paths, verify `git clone`/`push` work against a live wiki.
5. **Page REST API** — CRUD + frontmatter + per-file visibility, writing via plumbing.
6. **Renderer** — Jinja page render, markdown + frontmatter, wikilink resolver, `index.md` landing, `log.md` view.
7. **Search** — Postgres `tsvector` per page, scoped to wikis the viewer can see.
8. **Social** — fork (server-side clone), star, explore page, suggested edits (list + accept).
9. **MCP server** — thin wrapper over REST, shipped as `wikihost-mcp` npm/pip package.
10. **llms.txt surface** — each public wiki auto-generates `llms.txt` + serves raw `.md` with no chrome to LLM User-Agents. This is free but high-leverage for senses 1+3 of the research taxonomy.
11. **API docs page** — `/api/docs` with full endpoint reference (keep in sync; same rule as listhub's CLAUDE.md).
12. **Deploy + smoke test** — full round-trip: create wiki from web, push a page from git, point Claude Code at the MCP server, have it add a page, see it rendered, fork it from another account, suggest an edit.

Each milestone gets a verification task: `curl` the endpoint, `git clone` the wiki, screenshot the render, etc. Per your AGENTS.md rules.

## v2 tickets to create now (so we don't lose them)

1. **Paste-unstructured-text → LLM generates wiki.** Big textarea or file upload, server runs a coding-agent or Anthropic tool-use flow with *our* API key (not BYO). Open sub-decision: headless coding agent vs raw Anthropic API with a write-file tool. Lean: coding agent.
2. **Connect URL / existing public wiki.** Point at a GitHub repo or public URL, we clone/scrape into a new wiki under the user's account.
3. **Server-side agent, platform-provided key.** A "Run ingest / lint / query against this wiki" button in the web UI that runs a coding agent on our infra against the user's wiki. Extension of v2 ticket #1.
4. **Comments / discussion on wikis and pages.**
5. **D3 force-graph rendering of the wikilinks graph.**
6. **Inline diff UI for suggested edits** (v1 just lists them).
7. **Listing in the LLM Wikis directory** from your research doc — dogfood by registering wikihost itself as a sense-1 platform.

## Name (pick one, or veto and I'll propose more)

| Name | Angle | Downside |
|---|---|---|
| **wikihost** | Literal, Stripe-boring, easy to explain. "GitHub but for LLM wikis." | Generic, weak trademark. |
| **wikifork** | Leads with the social/collab wedge, which is your actual differentiator vs llmwiki.app/Quartz/Obsidian Publish. | Less clear what it *is* on first read. |

content.text:
Research subagent is running in the background — I'll fold its findings into the spec when it returns. Now the two things I can answer without waiting.

## 1. Git sync: this is the most important architectural question and I buried it. Fixing now.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- Listhub's model — and what I'm inheriting — is **bare git repo = source of truth, Postgres = derived index**. Not "Postgres with a git export" and not "git with a Postgres cache." The repo is the authoritative store; if you drop the database tomorrow, you can rebuild it by walking every repo's HEAD and reindexing. That's what makes `git clone` work as a legitimate primary interface, not a toy export.
- There are two sync directions and both run through the same bare repo: **DB→git** (web UI / REST writes commit via git plumbing with `GIT_INDEX_FILE`, no working tree) and **git→DB** (the `post-receive` hook parses changed `.md` files after a push and upserts Page rows via the admin API). The reason this doesn't infinite-loop is subtle and worth knowing: `git update-ref` (used by DB→git) does *not* fire hooks — only `git-receive-pack` (used by push) does. Listhub learned this the hard way; we inherit the fix.
- Because Postgres is derived, a nuclear "rebuild index from repo" command must exist from day one. Listhub has this (`python git_sync.py` rebuilds everything). We copy it.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

**So, concretely, the data architecture is:**

```
        ┌──────────────┐     writes via REST/Web
        │  Flask API   │ ────────────────────┐
        └──────┬───────┘                     │
               │ reads                       │ git plumbing
               │ (fast)                      │ (hash-object, update-index,
               ▼                             │  write-tree, commit-tree,
        ┌──────────────┐                     │  update-ref — NO hooks fire)
        │   Postgres   │                     │
        │  (derived)   │                     ▼
        │              │              ┌──────────────────┐
        │ wikis, pages │◀─── upsert ──│ repos/<u>/<w>.git│
        │ wikilinks    │   (admin     │  (source of      │
        │ tsvector     │    API)      │   truth)         │
        └──────────────┘              └────────▲─────────┘
                                               │
                                               │ git push / pull
                                               │
                                       ┌───────┴────────┐
                                       │   User /       │
                                       │  their agent   │
                                       └────────────────┘
                                               ▲
                                               │ post-receive hook
                                               │ (fires on push only)
                                               │ parses .md, calls admin API

Invariant: every page you can see in Postgres corresponds to a real blob at
HEAD in the bare repo. Postgres can be rebuilt from the repos at any time.
```

**Three operations that must exist on day one** (copied straight from listhub's playbook):
1. `wikihost reindex <wiki>` — rebuild Postgres rows for one wiki from `HEAD`.
2. `wikihost reindex --all` — nuke-and-rebuild everything.
3. `wikihost verify <wiki>` — diff Postgres vs. `HEAD`, report mismatches. Useful after any sync bug.

**What's in Postgres that's NOT in git:** social graph (stars, forks-of, suggested edits, follows), user accounts, API keys, sessions, page visibility *overrides* (see next point), search index. None of this belongs in the repo.

**Page visibility**: this is a design fork worth flagging. Two options:
- **(a) Visibility in frontmatter** — `visibility: private` in the YAML. Travels with the file on clone. Downside: a fork inherits the original's visibility labels, which may not be what the forker wants, and an attacker who can write to the repo via push can flip visibility.
- **(b) Visibility in Postgres only**, keyed by `(wiki_id, path)`. Clean ownership model, but `git clone` can't know a file is private from the blob alone — enforcement happens at the HTTP layer.
- **Recommendation: both, with Postgres authoritative.** Frontmatter carries a *hint* that gets copied into Postgres on write, but the DB row is what the serving layer checks. On push, post-receive reads the frontmatter and updates the DB row. On fork, we reset all visibility to the fork-owner's default (public). This matches how GitHub treats private-repo forks.

## 2. Agent-native signup, email-optional, programmatic rename

Folding this into the spec as a first-class concern, not an afterthought. Listhub already has username/display_name/email separation (I saw the recent `listhub-u02` commit in the git log — "user account rename: username/display_name/email"). We copy that split and go further.

**The rule:** an agent with nothing but a network connection and `curl` should be able to (1) create an account, (2) get an API key, (3) create a wiki, (4) start writing pages, **without a human ever opening a browser**. Every single step must have a REST endpoint and must not require email verification, CAPTCHA, or a cookie-based flow.

```
POST /api/v1/accounts                         # create account
  body: { username?, display_name?, email? }  # all optional
  returns: { user_id, username, api_key }     # username auto-generated if omitted
                                               # (adjective-noun-nanoid, like railway)

POST /api/v1/accounts/claim-email             # optional email affiliation
  auth: Bearer <api-key>
  body: { email }
  effect: sends verification link; on click, email is attached to the account
  rationale: email is an *affiliation*, not an identity. Account works without it.

PATCH /api/v1/accounts/me                     # programmatic rename
  auth: Bearer <api-key>
  body: { username?, display_name?, email? }
  effect:
    - username change: updates slug, all wiki URLs redirect from old → new for 90 days
    - display_name change: free, no side effects
    - email change: triggers re-verification if email was previously verified
  rationale: agents should be able to fix a typo in their own name without a human

POST /api/v1/accounts/me/api-keys              # rotate / create additional keys
DELETE /api/v1/accounts/me/api-keys/:id

DELETE /api/v1/accounts/me                     # programmatic account deletion
  (two-step: POST a confirmation token first, then DELETE with the token.
   Agents can do this without human help, but typos can't nuke an account.)
```

**Anti-abuse without breaking agents:**
- No CAPTCHA, no email verification to sign up. Instead: aggressive rate limits per IP on account creation (5/hour/IP), and a "karma" score — new accounts have reduced quotas (smaller wikis, stricter push rate limits) that lift after N days of non-abusive use. Standard pattern from Fly.io / Railway / Val Town.
- Abuse response is after-the-fact suspension, not prior restraint. Agents shouldn't be punished for being agents.

**Discovery:** `GET /.well-known/wikihost.json` returns `{ api_base, signup_endpoint, mcp_endpoint, docs }` so an agent pointed at the root domain can bootstrap itself without a human reading docs. This is the "self-describing site" pattern the research agent will report back on.

I'll expand this section once the research subagent returns with the WebMCP / .well-known / agent-auth findings — there's almost certainly a canonical manifest format I should align with.

- Agent-native auth has no standard. The working pattern: email-optional signup, API key return, PAT scopes, per-key rate limits, `X-Agent-Name` soft header, `actor` column in audit log. RFC 8693 delegation tokens for the future.
