1→# Agent-First Web Standards Brief — April 2026
     2→
     3→Scoped research input for a new LLM-wiki host that wants to be agent-native from day one. Not a survey — just what's actionable. Reuses prior research in `llm-wiki-research.md` and `llm-wikis-catalog.md` for llms.txt adopters and MCP-wiki peers; does not re-derive those.
     4→
     5→Verification caveat: everything below is synthesized from web search results performed 2026-04-08 from this session. Specs marked "proposed" or "SEP-####" are not yet merged into core standards. Where something is controversial I flag it explicitly.
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## 1. WebMCP
    10→
    11→**State of the art.** WebMCP is the current canonical answer to "how does a web page expose MCP tools to an agent in the browser." It is a W3C Web Machine Learning Community Group deliverable (accepted Sept 2025 after a Google/Microsoft unification in Aug 2025). Early preview shipped in Chrome 146 behind `chrome://flags` → "WebMCP for testing" in Feb 2026. Native Chrome/Edge support is targeted H2 2026. Not in Safari or Firefox.
    12→
    13→Two APIs:
    14→- **Declarative**: annotate HTML forms; browser exposes them as tools automatically.
    15→- **Imperative**: JS side — `navigator.modelContext.registerTool({name, description, inputSchema, handler})`. The browser's built-in agent (or an extension) can then invoke the tool in-page with the user's session cookies already attached.
    16→
    17→**Relationship to server-hosted MCP.** Orthogonal. Server MCP is for headless agents hitting your backend with their own credentials. WebMCP is for in-browser agents acting as the logged-in human, reusing the live session. Good sites will likely ship both: a server MCP for programmatic agents and WebMCP for the "Claude-in-my-browser" case. WebMCP notably sidesteps the auth problem for consumer agents because the user is already authenticated in the tab.
    18→
    19→**Discovery conventions.** No `.well-known` for WebMCP itself — tools are registered at runtime in the page. The loose convention settling in is: register tools early on page load, gate sensitive ones behind an explicit user-visible confirmation step (the spec emphasizes human-in-the-loop).
    20→
    21→**What "good" looks like.** A wiki page for an item would register at minimum: `read_page`, `edit_page(markdown)`, `append_section(heading, markdown)`, `add_tag(tag)`, `link_to(other_item_id)`. Input schemas are JSON Schema. Descriptions matter more than names — they're the only thing the LLM sees.
    22→
    23→**Unsettled/controversial.** (a) Safari/Firefox have said nothing public. (b) Extension-based agents vs. browser-builtin agents will compete for the same API surface; expect permission-prompt UX churn. (c) Spec still argues about whether tools should be enumerable across tabs or strictly per-tab. (d) No standard way yet to advertise "this site has WebMCP tools" before the agent loads the page — so discovery still falls back to llms.txt / well-known.
    24→
    25→**URLs.**
    26→- https://webmachinelearning.github.io/webmcp/ — the spec draft
    27→- https://github.com/webmachinelearning/webmcp — repo + issues
    28→- https://developer.chrome.com/blog/webmcp-epp — Chrome 146 early preview announcement
    29→- https://patrickbrosset.com/articles/2026-02-23-webmcp-updates-clarifications-and-next-steps/ — the clearest insider explainer
    30→- https://www.scalekit.com/blog/webmcp-the-missing-bridge-between-ai-agents-and-the-web — practical write-up with code
    31→
    32→---
    33→
    34→## 2. llms.txt
    35→
    36→**State of the art.** Still a proposed informal standard from AnswerDotAI. No W3C/IETF track. Directory counts as of early 2026: llmstxthub.com lists ~900 sites; llmstxt.io tracks ~780; the long tail (incl. small blogs) crosses 13,000 per catalog data in our prior research. The spec itself hasn't meaningfully changed since 2024.
    37→
    38→**The uncomfortable adoption data.** AEO/SearchSignal analyses in Q1 2026 found (a) <1% of pages cited by major AI answer engines have llms.txt; (b) no measurable citation uplift from having one; (c) none of OpenAI/Google/Anthropic/Meta/Mistral have publicly committed to consuming it. So it is culturally important — you will look unserious without it — but it is not actually a retrieval signal.
    39→
    40→**What's actually working in 2026.** The pattern that *is* getting traction is the sibling convention:
    41→- `/llms.txt` — curated index of key pages (links only, markdown)
    42→- `/llms-full.txt` — every page's markdown concatenated (Vercel ships ~400k words)
    43→- **Per-page `.md`**: serve `/some/page.md` alongside `/some/page`. This is the load-bearing pattern.
    44→- **Content negotiation**: see section 5. This is quietly eating the `.md` suffix convention's lunch because it doesn't require URL duplication.
    45→
    46→**What "good" looks like in April 2026.**
    47→1. `/llms.txt` as a curated table of contents grouped by section with an explicit `## Optional` bucket for low-priority pages (the Stripe pattern).
    48→2. `/llms-full.txt` auto-generated from your markdown store at build/deploy.
    49→3. Every canonical URL serves markdown via `Accept: text/markdown` *and* via `.md` suffix. Belt and suspenders.
    50→4. Frontmatter on every doc so the ingested `.md` carries its own metadata.
    51→5. **Do not** rely on llms.txt as your only agent surface. Pair it with content negotiation + a capability manifest.
    52→
    53→**Early-adopter examples** (already in `llm-wikis-catalog.md` — don't re-derive): Cloudflare, Stripe, Vercel, Supabase, Perplexity, Anthropic docs, Mintlify-hosted sites, GitBook, Fern.
    54→
    55→**Unsettled/controversial.** Mintlify/llms.txt proponents say "it's about supplying clean context on request, not SEO." Skeptics (Search Engine Land, ALLMO) counter "no major AI consumer actually reads it." Both are true. Treat it as table stakes for credibility, not as a distribution channel.
    56→
    57→**URLs.**
    58→- https://llmstxt.org/ — the spec
    59→- https://llmstxthub.com/ — the canonical directory
    60→- https://github.com/thedaviddias/llms-txt-hub — directory source
    61→- https://www.mintlify.com/blog/context-for-agents — content negotiation + llms.txt combined
    62→- https://www.aeo.press/ai/the-state-of-llms-txt-in-2026 — the skeptical data
    63→
    64→---
    65→
    66→## 3. Agent-native auth
    67→
    68→**State of the art.** No clean standard. Four patterns are in active use; pick them deliberately.
    69→
    70→1. **Bootstrap API key via signup endpoint.** `POST /api/v1/signup` → returns `{user_id, api_key}`. No email required, username optional (server-assigned if omitted). This is what ListHub already does and is the single cleanest pattern for a wiki host. The risk is abuse; see rate limiting below.
    71→
    72→2. **OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant (RFC 8628).** The "type this code into a browser" flow. Works well for CLI agents on the user's machine. Doesn't solve headless-agent signup — the user still has to exist.
    73→
    74→3. **WebAuthn + delegation.** Consensus in 2026 security circles (RSAC, Corbado, Bitwarden Agent Access SDK): **agents cannot and should not hold passkeys**. The pattern is human authenticates with passkey → server issues a scoped, short-lived delegation token for the agent. Bitwarden shipped an "Agent Access SDK" in early 2026 that intercepts the agent's API calls and injects keys from a vault so the LLM never sees them.
    75→
    76→4. **Token Exchange (RFC 8693) delegation tokens.** The formal answer to "agent acts on behalf of user." Token carries both subject (user) and actor (agent) identities. Very little consumer-facing tooling yet; mostly enterprise IAM (CyberArk, Okta).
    77→
    78→**Signup without email.** Becoming normal for agent-oriented services. Email as optional affiliation field — not required for account creation, only for "attach a human contact method later." GitHub-style username + programmatic `PATCH /users/me` to rename username/display_name/email is the emerging idiom. (ListHub already ships this — see `listhub-u02`.)
    79→
    80→**Rate limiting that doesn't break agents.** The working pattern:
    81→- Per-API-key quotas, not per-IP (agents share IPs with users).
    82→- Generous burst, tight sustained. 60 req/min burst, 1000 req/hour sustained is typical for wiki-scale writes.
    83→- `429` with `Retry-After` and a machine-readable JSON body `{error, retry_after_seconds, quota_remaining}` — agents handle this far better than HTML error pages.
    84→- Exempt read GETs of `.md` from aggressive limits — encourage scraping, it's the point.
    85→- Captchas on *signup* only, never on API calls. And a PoW/hashcash alternative for headless signup is worth offering as an escape hatch.
    86→
    87→**What "good" looks like for a wiki host.**
    88→- Signup: `POST /api/v1/signup {display_name?, email?}` → 201 `{user_id, username, api_key}`. No captcha if request carries a valid PoW token or invitation code.
    89→- Profile rename: `PATCH /api/v1/users/me` works for username, display_name, email independently.
    90→- Key management: `POST /api/v1/keys` (session-auth only), `DELETE /api/v1/keys/:id`, `GET /api/v1/keys`.
    91→- Treat API keys as PATs: one per agent/device, user-named, revocable, last-used timestamp.
    92→- Accept API key in Git HTTP Basic Auth alongside password (ListHub already does this).
    93→
    94→**Unsettled/controversial.** The biggest live debate: should an agent have its *own* identity distinct from the user (with its own `agent_id`), or should it only ever wear the user's identity via a token? Enterprise says "separate identity, audit trail required." Consumer tools say "too much friction, just use a scoped PAT." For a wiki host, start with scoped PATs and add agent-identity metadata (`X-Agent-Name` header, logged) as a non-enforced convention.
    95→
    96→**URLs.**
    97→- https://nango.dev/blog/guide-to-secure-ai-agent-api-authentication — best practical 2026 overview
    98→- https://www.corbado.com/blog/ai-agents-passkeys — why agents can't use passkeys, and the delegation pattern
    99→- https://bitwarden.com/blog/introducing-agent-access-sdk/ — vault-mediated key injection
   100→- https://developer.cyberark.com/blog/zero-trust-for-ai-agents-delegation-identity-and-access-control/ — RFC 8693 in practice
   101→- https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8628 — device flow
   102→
   103→---
   104→
   105→## 4. Agent-discoverable capability manifests
   106→
   107→**State of the art.** Fragmented. OpenAI's `ai-plugin.json` is dead. Its replacements haven't consolidated. As of Q1 2026 there are "104,000+ agents, 15+ registries, 10+ IETF drafts, zero interoperability" (global-chat.io's phrasing — harsh but fair).
   108→
   109→**The live candidates.**
   110→1. **MCP SEP-1649**: `/.well-known/mcp/server-card.json` — rich metadata (name, description, homepage, tools list, auth).
   111→2. **MCP SEP-1960**: `/.well-known/mcp` — RFC 8615 style endpoint enumeration + auth discovery, modeled on OAuth/OIDC discovery docs.
   112→3. **JSON Agents / PAM (jsonagents.org)**: portable agent manifest — JSON-Schema-based, framework-agnostic. Some traction, no major-vendor commitment.
   113→4. **AGENTS.md** (agents.md, adopted by OpenAI Codex, Anthropic Claude Code, Cursor): plain Markdown at the repo/site root telling *coding* agents how to work with the codebase. Not a capability manifest in the RPC sense — it's prose instructions. But it is the *one* format that has actually achieved multi-vendor adoption in 2026, so ship it.
   114→5. **OpenAPI + `x-mcp` extensions**: some sites annotate their existing OpenAPI spec with MCP hints. Low ceremony; works today.
   115→
   116→**Neither SEP-1649 nor SEP-1960 is merged** into core MCP spec as of Feb 2026. Discussion lives at https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/modelcontextprotocol/discussions/1147. Major clients (Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT) are reportedly implementing both speculatively. The advice from the Ekamoira write-up is "ship both endpoints."
   117→
   118→**What "good" looks like for a wiki host in April 2026.** Ship all of these — they're cheap:
   119→1. `/.well-known/mcp/server-card.json` (SEP-1649 shape) — rich metadata, link to `mcp_endpoint`.
   120→2. `/.well-known/mcp` (SEP-1960 shape) — endpoint + auth info.
   121→3. `/AGENTS.md` at site root — prose instructions: "this is a wiki host, here's how to sign up, here's where the API is, here's llms.txt, here's the MCP server URL."
   122→4. Existing OpenAPI at `/openapi.json` with a clear link from llms.txt.
   123→5. Link all of these from `/llms.txt`.
   124→
   125→**Unsettled/controversial.** (a) Whether capability discovery should be *in* MCP's domain or a separate protocol. (b) Whether agents should trust a site's self-declared capability list at all (risk of prompt injection via tool descriptions). (c) Whether the manifest should live at `/.well-known/` or be an HTTP response header. Current vibe: `.well-known` wins because it's cacheable and doesn't need per-request logic.
   126→
   127→**URLs.**
   128→- https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/modelcontextprotocol/discussions/1147 — SEP discussion
   129→- https://www.ekamoira.com/blog/mcp-server-discovery-implement-well-known-mcp-json-2026-guide — implementation guide
   130→- https://global-chat.io/discovery-landscape — "State of Agent Discovery Q1 2026"
   131→- https://agents.md/ — AGENTS.md standard
   132→- https://jsonagents.org/getting-started/ — the competing PAM manifest
   133→- https://developers.openai.com/codex/guides/agents-md — OpenAI Codex AGENTS.md adoption
   134→
   135→---
   136→
   137→## 5. Agent-friendly rendering conventions
   138→
   139→**State of the art.** Content negotiation via `Accept: text/markdown` is the winning pattern of 2026. This is the single most important section of this brief — if you only do one thing, do this.
   140→
   141→**Established as of Q1 2026:**
   142→- **Vercel** confirmed (Feb 2026) that Claude Code and OpenCode send `Accept: text/markdown` on every fetch. Vercel now responds with markdown automatically on matching routes.
   143→- **Cloudflare Markdown for Agents** is a zone-level feature that auto-converts HTML→markdown on the edge when the client sends `Accept: text/markdown`. Same URL, different representation. Available Pro/Biz/Enterprise.
   144→- **Mintlify** serves per-page markdown via both content negotiation and `.md` suffix fallback.
   145→- **Measured wins**: Cloudflare's internal measurement shows ~80% token reduction (16k → 3k tokens on a typical article). One Eleventy write-up reported 99.6% size reduction on very HTML-heavy pages.
   146→
   147→**The URL-rewriting pattern** (r.jina.ai, deepwiki.com).
   148→- `r.jina.ai/<url>` — prepend to any URL, get clean markdown. Agent-oblivious sites get "agentified" for free.
   149→- `deepwiki.com/<github-path>` — auto-generated wiki view of any public GitHub repo. Used by agents as a pre-rendered summary layer.
   150→- Emerging informal norm: in agent prompts, swap `github.com` → `deepwiki.com` and swap arbitrary URLs → `r.jina.ai/<url>` before fetching. This is a *client-side* convention, not a server one, but it exists because most sites don't serve markdown themselves.
   151→- **Implication for a new site**: serve markdown natively and this whole indirection layer disappears. You are the authoritative agent view.
   152→
   153→**JSON-LD / schema.org.** Still useful for structured facts (author, datePublished, license) that markdown doesn't express cleanly. Continue emitting it — LLMs do consume it, even if they prefer markdown prose.
   154→
   155→**What "good" looks like for a wiki host.**
   156→1. Every item URL responds to `Accept: text/markdown` with the raw item markdown + frontmatter. Zero HTML.
   157→2. Every item URL also serves at `<url>.md` as a fallback (for clients that don't negotiate). Cheap and bulletproof.
   158→3. HTML version includes JSON-LD with `@type: Article`, author, dates, license.
   159→4. `Link: <...>; rel="alternate"; type="text/markdown"` header on HTML responses — formally declares the markdown alt.
   160→5. Don't gzip markdown responses differently than HTML; agents handle both fine but HTTP caches are cleaner if you match your HTML path.
   161→6. `Vary: Accept` header on all content routes so caches don't mix HTML and markdown.
   162→
   163→**Unsettled/controversial.** (a) Whether to also honor `Accept: application/json` with a schema.org-shaped response. Some sites do; it adds maintenance. (b) Whether markdown should be "the authored source" or a derived view. For a wiki host, markdown IS the source — trivial. (c) Some argue for `Accept: text/x-markdown` or a `profile` parameter; the pragmatic consensus is plain `text/markdown`.
   164→
   165→**URLs.**
   166→- https://blog.cloudflare.com/markdown-for-agents/ — the canonical 2026 write-up
   167→- https://vercel.com/blog/making-agent-friendly-pages-with-content-negotiation — Vercel's rollout
   168→- https://www.checklyhq.com/blog/state-of-ai-agent-content-negotation/ — Feb 2026 state-of-the-art
   169→- https://github.com/jina-ai/reader — r.jina.ai Reader, the universal fallback
   170→- https://www.mintlify.com/blog/context-for-agents — llms.txt + negotiation together
   171→
   172→---
   173→
   174→## 6. Session/identity for agents
   175→
   176→**State of the art.** Still forming. No "agent identity" standard has shipped. What exists:
   177→
   178→- **RFC 8693 Token Exchange** — the formal primitive for "act on behalf of." Enterprise IAM supports it. Consumer tooling mostly doesn't.
   179→- **Anthropic's position (Feb 2026)** — explicitly prohibits using Claude Free/Pro/Max OAuth tokens in third-party products. Developers must use API keys from Console. Translation: Anthropic is *not* shipping a consumer agent-identity layer and is actively rejecting the idea of "delegate my Claude account." This is a significant signal — it pushes the industry toward the per-site PAT model rather than a universal agent identity.
   180→- **Signed agent assertions** — no live standard. Some drafts propose signed JWTs where the agent's public key is registered at a well-known endpoint, then the agent signs each request. Not adopted.
   181→- **PAT vs delegation token distinction.** PAT = long-lived, user-scoped, coarse, all-or-nothing. Delegation token = short-lived, carries both subject and actor IDs, scoped to specific actions, audit-friendly. For a wiki host the pragmatic call is: ship PATs now, add a `POST /api/v1/delegation` endpoint later that mints short-lived scoped tokens from a PAT.
   182→
   183→**What "good" looks like in April 2026.** Accept that agent identity is an open problem and design so you can add it later:
   184→1. Ship user-scoped PATs as the default agent credential.
   185→2. Log a soft `X-Agent-Name` / `X-Agent-Version` header on every API call. Don't enforce it, but surface it in the user's dashboard ("this key was used by `claude-code@1.2.3`"). Users love this.
   186→3. Put an `actor` field in your audit log from day one, even if it's always null now. When RFC 8693 becomes mainstream you'll have the schema already.
   187→4. Per-key scopes (`read`, `write`, `admin`) even if you ship with only one scope. You'll want them.
   188→5. Per-key rate limits distinct from per-user limits.
   189→
   190→**Unsettled/controversial.** (a) Whether the agent's identity should be visible to other users ("edited by alice via claude-code") — privacy vs. transparency tension. Recommend opt-in visibility. (b) Whether to let users create a "shared agent identity" that multiple humans can delegate to — the multi-human-one-agent case is messy and mostly unsolved.
   191→
   192→**URLs.**
   193→- https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8693 — the primitive
   194→- https://developer.cyberark.com/blog/zero-trust-for-ai-agents-delegation-identity-and-access-control/ — practical delegation patterns
   195→- https://www.biometricupdate.com/202603/ai-agent-identity-and-next-gen-enterprise-authentication-prominent-at-rsac-2026 — RSAC 2026 roundup
   196→- https://medium.com/@em.mcconnell/the-missing-piece-in-anthropics-ecosystem-third-party-oauth-ccb5addb8810 — the Anthropic OAuth restriction story
   197→- https://nango.dev/blog/guide-to-secure-ai-agent-api-authentication — 2026 end-to-end guide
   198→
   199→---
   200→
   201→## 7. Directories, scenes, and voices
   202→
   203→**The loudest voices on "agent-first web" in early 2026.**
   204→- **Patrick Brosset** (ex-Microsoft Edge, WebMCP co-spec author) — most credible technical voice on WebMCP. https://patrickbrosset.com/
   205→- **Alex Nahas** (Arcade.dev) — pragmatic WebMCP/tool-calling commentary. https://www.arcade.dev/blog/
   206→- **Cloudflare blog** (Markdown for Agents, robots.txt for AI) — de facto infrastructure voice.
   207→- **Mintlify blog** — docs-sector voice for llms.txt + content negotiation.
   208→- **Vercel blog** — "agent-friendly pages" post is becoming the canonical "how we retrofitted" story.
   209→- **David Dias** — runs llmstxthub, best neutral curator of llms.txt ecosystem.
   210→- **Rob Manson** (Medium, "Re-defining the Agentic Web", Feb 2026) — the "everyone's building the wrong thing" contrarian.
   211→- **IEEE Spectrum "The Agentic Web"** — the mainstream-press framing.
   212→
   213→**Manifestos worth skimming.**
   214→- https://unanimoustech.com/web-development-manifesto-2026/ — "2026 Web Development Manifesto"
   215→- https://dev.to/xh1m/the-architects-manifesto-2026-engineering-in-the-age-of-autonomy-54dc — "Architect's Manifesto 2026"
   216→- https://www.nxcode.io/resources/news/agentic-web-agents-md-mcp-a2a-web-4-guide-2026 — best single synthesis of AGENTS.md + MCP + A2A
   217→- https://medium.com/data-science-collective/re-defining-the-agentic-web-cfeb49e0d4e7 — the skeptic's take
   218→- https://spectrum.ieee.org/agentic-web — the IEEE Spectrum overview
   219→
   220→**Standards bodies active in the space.**
   221→- **W3C Web Machine Learning CG** — owns WebMCP draft. Most legitimate standards venue in the space.
   222→- **Anthropic MCP governance** — the SEP process, GitHub-hosted, not a standards body but functionally setting norms.
   223→- **IETF** — 10+ agent-discovery drafts, all early, none adopted. Don't design against any of them.
   224→- **OpenAI** — no standards push post-ai-plugin.json except their AGENTS.md adoption.
   225→- **Google** — pushing A2A (Agent-to-Agent) as a sibling to MCP; not directly relevant to a wiki host.
   226→
   227→**Directories to register in when launching.**
   228→- https://llmstxthub.com/ — for llms.txt compliance (~900 sites)
   229→- https://mcp.so/ and https://smithery.ai/ — MCP server directories (primary)
   230→- https://mcpservers.org/ — claims 20k, lower-quality
   231→- https://github.com/thedaviddias/llms-txt-hub — source-of-truth for llmstxthub
   232→- https://agents.md/ registry — AGENTS.md adopters list
   233→
   234→---
   235→
   236→## Recommended minimum for ListHub's new wiki host (action list)
   237→
   238→Ordered by leverage-per-hour. Every item maps to a section above.
   239→
   240→1. **Content negotiation on every item URL.** `Accept: text/markdown` → raw markdown. Also `<url>.md` suffix fallback. `Vary: Accept` and `Link: rel=alternate`. *(§5)* This is the highest-leverage item on the list.
   241→2. **`/llms.txt` + `/llms-full.txt`** auto-generated from the item store. Curated top-level, optional section for long tail. *(§2)*
   242→3. **`/AGENTS.md` at site root** explaining signup, API, MCP, llms.txt. Prose, short, link-heavy. *(§4)*
   243→4. **`/.well-known/mcp/server-card.json`** and **`/.well-known/mcp`**. Ship both SEP shapes; they're cheap and they future-proof you. *(§4)*
   244→5. **Server-hosted MCP server** wrapping the existing REST API. Tools: `search`, `read_item`, `create_item`, `update_item`, `tag_item`, `list_items_by_user`. *(§1, §4)*
   245→6. **Signup endpoint that returns an API key**, email optional, PoW fallback to captcha, `X-Agent-Name` soft header logged. *(§3)*
   246→7. **WebMCP tool registration** on item edit pages: `read_page`, `edit_page`, `append_section`, `add_tag`. Ship behind feature-detect. *(§1)*
   247→8. **Per-API-key scopes + per-key rate limits + `actor` column in audit log**. Even if unused today. *(§3, §6)*
   248→9. **Delegation endpoint (`POST /api/v1/delegation`)** that mints short-lived scoped tokens from a PAT. Ship the shell now, the semantics can evolve. *(§6)*
   249→10. **Register in llmstxthub, mcp.so, Smithery** at launch. *(§7)*
   250→
   251→---
   252→
   253→## What I verified and what I couldn't
   254→
   255→Verified:
   256→- All section-level claims are backed by live Q1 2026 search results (searches run 2026-04-08 in this session).
   257→- Existing ListHub research (`llm-wiki-research.md`, `llm-wikis-catalog.md`) was checked and reused for llms.txt adopter lists and MCP-wiki peer list — not re-derived.
   258→- URLs listed were all returned by WebSearch; I did not individually WebFetch each to confirm status codes.
   259→
   260→Not verified / gaps:
   261→- I did **not** confirm Chrome 146 WebMCP flag behavior on a real browser. The source is a Chrome developer blog post summary.
   262→- SEP-1649/SEP-1960 exact field schemas — I have the shape, not line-by-line spec. Before implementing, read the GitHub discussion directly (link in §4).
   263→- Cloudflare Markdown for Agents pricing/tier details — I have "Pro/Biz/Enterprise" from one source; verify on Cloudflare's pricing page before committing.
   264→- Exact current count of llms.txt adopters — cited figures (900, 780, 13k) come from different directories with different inclusion criteria; none are authoritative.
   265→- Anthropic's Feb 2026 OAuth policy — I have it from a Medium post and a GitHub issue, not from the Anthropic policy page itself. Confirm before citing externally.
   266→- WebAuthn-for-agents is described as "consensus" — it's consensus *among security vendors and RSAC speakers*, not a formal W3C position.
   267→
   268→If you want, next step is to WebFetch the top 3-5 spec sources directly (WebMCP draft, SEP-1649/1960 discussion, llms.txt spec, Cloudflare Markdown for Agents post) and pull exact schemas/field names into a second, implementation-ready doc.

# Agent-First Web Standards Brief — April 2026

Scoped research input for a new LLM-wiki host that wants to be agent-native from day one. Not a survey — just what's actionable. Reuses prior research in `llm-wiki-research.md` and `llm-wikis-catalog.md` for llms.txt adopters and MCP-wiki peers; does not re-derive those.

Verification caveat: everything below is synthesized from web search results performed 2026-04-08 from this session. Specs marked "proposed" or "SEP-####" are not yet merged into core standards. Where something is controversial I flag it explicitly.

---

## 1. WebMCP

**State of the art.** WebMCP is the current canonical answer to "how does a web page expose MCP tools to an agent in the browser." It is a W3C Web Machine Learning Community Group deliverable (accepted Sept 2025 after a Google/Microsoft unification in Aug 2025). Early preview shipped in Chrome 146 behind `chrome://flags` → "WebMCP for testing" in Feb 2026. Native Chrome/Edge support is targeted H2 2026. Not in Safari or Firefox.

Two APIs:
- **Declarative**: annotate HTML forms; browser exposes them as tools automatically.
- **Imperative**: JS side — `navigator.modelContext.registerTool({name, description, inputSchema, handler})`. The browser's built-in agent (or an extension) can then invoke the tool in-page with the user's session cookies already attached.

**Relationship to server-hosted MCP.** Orthogonal. Server MCP is for headless agents hitting your backend with their own credentials. WebMCP is for in-browser agents acting as the logged-in human, reusing the live session. Good sites will likely ship both: a server MCP for programmatic agents and WebMCP for the "Claude-in-my-browser" case. WebMCP notably sidesteps the auth problem for consumer agents because the user is already authenticated in the tab.

**Discovery conventions.** No `.well-known` for WebMCP itself — tools are registered at runtime in the page. The loose convention settling in is: register tools early on page load, gate sensitive ones behind an explicit user-visible confirmation step (the spec emphasizes human-in-the-loop).

**What "good" looks like.** A wiki page for an item would register at minimum: `read_page`, `edit_page(markdown)`, `append_section(heading, markdown)`, `add_tag(tag)`, `link_to(other_item_id)`. Input schemas are JSON Schema. Descriptions matter more than names — they're the only thing the LLM sees.

**Unsettled/controversial.** (a) Safari/Firefox have said nothing public. (b) Extension-based agents vs. browser-builtin agents will compete for the same API surface; expect permission-prompt UX churn. (c) Spec still argues about whether tools should be enumerable across tabs or strictly per-tab. (d) No standard way yet to advertise "this site has WebMCP tools" before the agent loads the page — so discovery still falls back to llms.txt / well-known.

**URLs.**
- https://webmachinelearning.github.io/webmcp/ — the spec draft
- https://github.com/webmachinelearning/webmcp — repo + issues
- https://developer.chrome.com/blog/webmcp-epp — Chrome 146 early preview announcement
- https://patrickbrosset.com/articles/2026-02-23-webmcp-updates-clarifications-and-next-steps/ — the clearest insider explainer
- https://www.scalekit.com/blog/webmcp-the-missing-bridge-between-ai-agents-and-the-web — practical write-up with code

---

## 2. llms.txt

**State of the art.** Still a proposed informal standard from AnswerDotAI. No W3C/IETF track. Directory counts as of early 2026: llmstxthub.com lists ~900 sites; llmstxt.io tracks ~780; the long tail (incl. small blogs) crosses 13,000 per catalog data in our prior research. The spec itself hasn't meaningfully changed since 2024.

**The uncomfortable adoption data.** AEO/SearchSignal analyses in Q1 2026 found (a) <1% of pages cited by major AI answer engines have llms.txt; (b) no measurable citation uplift from having one; (c) none of OpenAI/Google/Anthropic/Meta/Mistral have publicly committed to consuming it. So it is culturally important — you will look unserious without it — but it is not actually a retrieval signal.

**What's actually working in 2026.** The pattern that *is* getting traction is the sibling convention:
- `/llms.txt` — curated index of key pages (links only, markdown)
- `/llms-full.txt` — every page's markdown concatenated (Vercel ships ~400k words)
- **Per-page `.md`**: serve `/some/page.md` alongside `/some/page`. This is the load-bearing pattern.
- **Content negotiation**: see section 5. This is quietly eating the `.md` suffix convention's lunch because it doesn't require URL duplication.

**What "good" looks like in April 2026.**
1. `/llms.txt` as a curated table of contents grouped by section with an explicit `## Optional` bucket for low-priority pages (the Stripe pattern).
2. `/llms-full.txt` auto-generated from your markdown store at build/deploy.
3. Every canonical URL serves markdown via `Accept: text/markdown` *and* via `.md` suffix. Belt and suspenders.
4. Frontmatter on every doc so the ingested `.md` carries its own metadata.
5. **Do not** rely on llms.txt as your only agent surface. Pair it with content negotiation + a capability manifest.

**Early-adopter examples** (already in `llm-wikis-catalog.md` — don't re-derive): Cloudflare, Stripe, Vercel, Supabase, Perplexity, Anthropic docs, Mintlify-hosted sites, GitBook, Fern.

**Unsettled/controversial.** Mintlify/llms.txt proponents say "it's about supplying clean context on request, not SEO." Skeptics (Search Engine Land, ALLMO) counter "no major AI consumer actually reads it." Both are true. Treat it as table stakes for credibility, not as a distribution channel.

**URLs.**
- https://llmstxt.org/ — the spec
- https://llmstxthub.com/ — the canonical directory
- https://github.com/thedaviddias/llms-txt-hub — directory source
- https://www.mintlify.com/blog/context-for-agents — content negotiation + llms.txt combined
- https://www.aeo.press/ai/the-state-of-llms-txt-in-2026 — the skeptical data

---

## 3. Agent-native auth

**State of the art.** No clean standard. Four patterns are in active use; pick them deliberately.

1. **Bootstrap API key via signup endpoint.** `POST /api/v1/signup` → returns `{user_id, api_key}`. No email required, username optional (server-assigned if omitted). This is what ListHub already does and is the single cleanest pattern for a wiki host. The risk is abuse; see rate limiting below.

2. **OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant (RFC 8628).** The "type this code into a browser" flow. Works well for CLI agents on the user's machine. Doesn't solve headless-agent signup — the user still has to exist.

3. **WebAuthn + delegation.** Consensus in 2026 security circles (RSAC, Corbado, Bitwarden Agent Access SDK): **agents cannot and should not hold passkeys**. The pattern is human authenticates with passkey → server issues a scoped, short-lived delegation token for the agent. Bitwarden shipped an "Agent Access SDK" in early 2026 that intercepts the agent's API calls and injects keys from a vault so the LLM never sees them.

4. **Token Exchange (RFC 8693) delegation tokens.** The formal answer to "agent acts on behalf of user." Token carries both subject (user) and actor (agent) identities. Very little consumer-facing tooling yet; mostly enterprise IAM (CyberArk, Okta).

**Signup without email.** Becoming normal for agent-oriented services. Email as optional affiliation field — not required for account creation, only for "attach a human contact method later." GitHub-style username + programmatic `PATCH /users/me` to rename username/display_name/email is the emerging idiom. (ListHub already ships this — see `listhub-u02`.)

**Rate limiting that doesn't break agents.** The working pattern:
- Per-API-key quotas, not per-IP (agents share IPs with users).
- Generous burst, tight sustained. 60 req/min burst, 1000 req/hour sustained is typical for wiki-scale writes.
- `429` with `Retry-After` and a machine-readable JSON body `{error, retry_after_seconds, quota_remaining}` — agents handle this far better than HTML error pages.
- Exempt read GETs of `.md` from aggressive limits — encourage scraping, it's the point.
- Captchas on *signup* only, never on API calls. And a PoW/hashcash alternative for headless signup is worth offering as an escape hatch.

**What "good" looks like for a wiki host.**
- Signup: `POST /api/v1/signup {display_name?, email?}` → 201 `{user_id, username, api_key}`. No captcha if request carries a valid PoW token or invitation code.
- Profile rename: `PATCH /api/v1/users/me` works for username, display_name, email independently.
- Key management: `POST /api/v1/keys` (session-auth only), `DELETE /api/v1/keys/:id`, `GET /api/v1/keys`.
- Treat API keys as PATs: one per agent/device, user-named, revocable, last-used timestamp.
- Accept API key in Git HTTP Basic Auth alongside password (ListHub already does this).

**Unsettled/controversial.** The biggest live debate: should an agent have its *own* identity distinct from the user (with its own `agent_id`), or should it only ever wear the user's identity via a token? Enterprise says "separate identity, audit trail required." Consumer tools say "too much friction, just use a scoped PAT." For a wiki host, start with scoped PATs and add agent-identity metadata (`X-Agent-Name` header, logged) as a non-enforced convention.

**URLs.**
- https://nango.dev/blog/guide-to-secure-ai-agent-api-authentication — best practical 2026 overview
- https://www.corbado.com/blog/ai-agents-passkeys — why agents can't use passkeys, and the delegation pattern
- https://bitwarden.com/blog/introducing-agent-access-sdk/ — vault-mediated key injection
- https://developer.cyberark.com/blog/zero-trust-for-ai-agents-delegation-identity-and-access-control/ — RFC 8693 in practice
- https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8628 — device flow

---

## 4. Agent-discoverable capability manifests

**State of the art.** Fragmented. OpenAI's `ai-plugin.json` is dead. Its replacements haven't consolidated. As of Q1 2026 there are "104,000+ agents, 15+ registries, 10+ IETF drafts, zero interoperability" (global-chat.io's phrasing — harsh but fair).

**The live candidates.**
1. **MCP SEP-1649**: `/.well-known/mcp/server-card.json` — rich metadata (name, description, homepage, tools list, auth).
2. **MCP SEP-1960**: `/.well-known/mcp` — RFC 8615 style endpoint enumeration + auth discovery, modeled on OAuth/OIDC discovery docs.
3. **JSON Agents / PAM (jsonagents.org)**: portable agent manifest — JSON-Schema-based, framework-agnostic. Some traction, no major-vendor commitment.
4. **AGENTS.md** (agents.md, adopted by OpenAI Codex, Anthropic Claude Code, Cursor): plain Markdown at the repo/site root telling *coding* agents how to work with the codebase. Not a capability manifest in the RPC sense — it's prose instructions. But it is the *one* format that has actually achieved multi-vendor adoption in 2026, so ship it.
5. **OpenAPI + `x-mcp` extensions**: some sites annotate their existing OpenAPI spec with MCP hints. Low ceremony; works today.

**Neither SEP-1649 nor SEP-1960 is merged** into core MCP spec as of Feb 2026. Discussion lives at https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/modelcontextprotocol/discussions/1147. Major clients (Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT) are reportedly implementing both speculatively. The advice from the Ekamoira write-up is "ship both endpoints."

**What "good" looks like for a wiki host in April 2026.** Ship all of these — they're cheap:
1. `/.well-known/mcp/server-card.json` (SEP-1649 shape) — rich metadata, link to `mcp_endpoint`.
2. `/.well-known/mcp` (SEP-1960 shape) — endpoint + auth info.
3. `/AGENTS.md` at site root — prose instructions: "this is a wiki host, here's how to sign up, here's where the API is, here's llms.txt, here's the MCP server URL."
4. Existing OpenAPI at `/openapi.json` with a clear link from llms.txt.
5. Link all of these from `/llms.txt`.

**Unsettled/controversial.** (a) Whether capability discovery should be *in* MCP's domain or a separate protocol. (b) Whether agents should trust a site's self-declared capability list at all (risk of prompt injection via tool descriptions). (c) Whether the manifest should live at `/.well-known/` or be an HTTP response header. Current vibe: `.well-known` wins because it's cacheable and doesn't need per-request logic.

**URLs.**
- https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/modelcontextprotocol/discussions/1147 — SEP discussion
- https://www.ekamoira.com/blog/mcp-server-discovery-implement-well-known-mcp-json-2026-guide — implementation guide
- https://global-chat.io/discovery-landscape — "State of Agent Discovery Q1 2026"
- https://agents.md/ — AGENTS.md standard
- https://jsonagents.org/getting-started/ — the competing PAM manifest
- https://developers.openai.com/codex/guides/agents-md — OpenAI Codex AGENTS.md adoption

---

## 5. Agent-friendly rendering conventions

**State of the art.** Content negotiation via `Accept: text/markdown` is the winning pattern of 2026. This is the single most important section of this brief — if you only do one thing, do this.

**Established as of Q1 2026:**
- **Vercel** confirmed (Feb 2026) that Claude Code and OpenCode send `Accept: text/markdown` on every fetch. Vercel now responds with markdown automatically on matching routes.
- **Cloudflare Markdown for Agents** is a zone-level feature that auto-converts HTML→markdown on the edge when the client sends `Accept: text/markdown`. Same URL, different representation. Available Pro/Biz/Enterprise.
- **Mintlify** serves per-page markdown via both content negotiation and `.md` suffix fallback.
- **Measured wins**: Cloudflare's internal measurement shows ~80% token reduction (16k → 3k tokens on a typical article). One Eleventy write-up reported 99.6% size reduction on very HTML-heavy pages.

**The URL-rewriting pattern** (r.jina.ai, deepwiki.com).
- `r.jina.ai/<url>` — prepend to any URL, get clean markdown. Agent-oblivious sites get "agentified" for free.
- `deepwiki.com/<github-path>` — auto-generated wiki view of any public GitHub repo. Used by agents as a pre-rendered summary layer.
- Emerging informal norm: in agent prompts, swap `github.com` → `deepwiki.com` and swap arbitrary URLs → `r.jina.ai/<url>` before fetching. This is a *client-side* convention, not a server one, but it exists because most sites don't serve markdown themselves.
- **Implication for a new site**: serve markdown natively and this whole indirection layer disappears. You are the authoritative agent view.

**JSON-LD / schema.org.** Still useful for structured facts (author, datePublished, license) that markdown doesn't express cleanly. Continue emitting it — LLMs do consume it, even if they prefer markdown prose.

**What "good" looks like for a wiki host.**
1. Every item URL responds to `Accept: text/markdown` with the raw item markdown + frontmatter. Zero HTML.
2. Every item URL also serves at `<url>.md` as a fallback (for clients that don't negotiate). Cheap and bulletproof.
3. HTML version includes JSON-LD with `@type: Article`, author, dates, license.
4. `Link: <...>; rel="alternate"; type="text/markdown"` header on HTML responses — formally declares the markdown alt.
5. Don't gzip markdown responses differently than HTML; agents handle both fine but HTTP caches are cleaner if you match your HTML path.
6. `Vary: Accept` header on all content routes so caches don't mix HTML and markdown.

**Unsettled/controversial.** (a) Whether to also honor `Accept: application/json` with a schema.org-shaped response. Some sites do; it adds maintenance. (b) Whether markdown should be "the authored source" or a derived view. For a wiki host, markdown IS the source — trivial. (c) Some argue for `Accept: text/x-markdown` or a `profile` parameter; the pragmatic consensus is plain `text/markdown`.

**URLs.**
- https://blog.cloudflare.com/markdown-for-agents/ — the canonical 2026 write-up
- https://vercel.com/blog/making-agent-friendly-pages-with-content-negotiation — Vercel's rollout
- https://www.checklyhq.com/blog/state-of-ai-agent-content-negotation/ — Feb 2026 state-of-the-art
- https://github.com/jina-ai/reader — r.jina.ai Reader, the universal fallback
- https://www.mintlify.com/blog/context-for-agents — llms.txt + negotiation together

---

## 6. Session/identity for agents

**State of the art.** Still forming. No "agent identity" standard has shipped. What exists:

- **RFC 8693 Token Exchange** — the formal primitive for "act on behalf of." Enterprise IAM supports it. Consumer tooling mostly doesn't.
- **Anthropic's position (Feb 2026)** — explicitly prohibits using Claude Free/Pro/Max OAuth tokens in third-party products. Developers must use API keys from Console. Translation: Anthropic is *not* shipping a consumer agent-identity layer and is actively rejecting the idea of "delegate my Claude account." This is a significant signal — it pushes the industry toward the per-site PAT model rather than a universal agent identity.
- **Signed agent assertions** — no live standard. Some drafts propose signed JWTs where the agent's public key is registered at a well-known endpoint, then the agent signs each request. Not adopted.
- **PAT vs delegation token distinction.** PAT = long-lived, user-scoped, coarse, all-or-nothing. Delegation token = short-lived, carries both subject and actor IDs, scoped to specific actions, audit-friendly. For a wiki host the pragmatic call is: ship PATs now, add a `POST /api/v1/delegation` endpoint later that mints short-lived scoped tokens from a PAT.

**What "good" looks like in April 2026.** Accept that agent identity is an open problem and design so you can add it later:
1. Ship user-scoped PATs as the default agent credential.
2. Log a soft `X-Agent-Name` / `X-Agent-Version` header on every API call. Don't enforce it, but surface it in the user's dashboard ("this key was used by `claude-code@1.2.3`"). Users love this.
3. Put an `actor` field in your audit log from day one, even if it's always null now. When RFC 8693 becomes mainstream you'll have the schema already.
4. Per-key scopes (`read`, `write`, `admin`) even if you ship with only one scope. You'll want them.
5. Per-key rate limits distinct from per-user limits.

**Unsettled/controversial.** (a) Whether the agent's identity should be visible to other users ("edited by alice via claude-code") — privacy vs. transparency tension. Recommend opt-in visibility. (b) Whether to let users create a "shared agent identity" that multiple humans can delegate to — the multi-human-one-agent case is messy and mostly unsolved.

**URLs.**
- https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8693 — the primitive
- https://developer.cyberark.com/blog/zero-trust-for-ai-agents-delegation-identity-and-access-control/ — practical delegation patterns
- https://www.biometricupdate.com/202603/ai-agent-identity-and-next-gen-enterprise-authentication-prominent-at-rsac-2026 — RSAC 2026 roundup
- https://medium.com/@em.mcconnell/the-missing-piece-in-anthropics-ecosystem-third-party-oauth-ccb5addb8810 — the Anthropic OAuth restriction story
- https://nango.dev/blog/guide-to-secure-ai-agent-api-authentication — 2026 end-to-end guide

---

## 7. Directories, scenes, and voices

**The loudest voices on "agent-first web" in early 2026.**
- **Patrick Brosset** (ex-Microsoft Edge, WebMCP co-spec author) — most credible technical voice on WebMCP. https://patrickbrosset.com/
- **Alex Nahas** (Arcade.dev) — pragmatic WebMCP/tool-calling commentary. https://www.arcade.dev/blog/
- **Cloudflare blog** (Markdown for Agents, robots.txt for AI) — de facto infrastructure voice.
- **Mintlify blog** — docs-sector voice for llms.txt + content negotiation.
- **Vercel blog** — "agent-friendly pages" post is becoming the canonical "how we retrofitted" story.
- **David Dias** — runs llmstxthub, best neutral curator of llms.txt ecosystem.
- **Rob Manson** (Medium, "Re-defining the Agentic Web", Feb 2026) — the "everyone's building the wrong thing" contrarian.
- **IEEE Spectrum "The Agentic Web"** — the mainstream-press framing.

**Manifestos worth skimming.**
- https://unanimoustech.com/web-development-manifesto-2026/ — "2026 Web Development Manifesto"
- https://dev.to/xh1m/the-architects-manifesto-2026-engineering-in-the-age-of-autonomy-54dc — "Architect's Manifesto 2026"
- https://www.nxcode.io/resources/news/agentic-web-agents-md-mcp-a2a-web-4-guide-2026 — best single synthesis of AGENTS.md + MCP + A2A
- https://medium.com/data-science-collective/re-defining-the-agentic-web-cfeb49e0d4e7 — the skeptic's take
- https://spectrum.ieee.org/agentic-web — the IEEE Spectrum overview

**Standards bodies active in the space.**
- **W3C Web Machine Learning CG** — owns WebMCP draft. Most legitimate standards venue in the space.
- **Anthropic MCP governance** — the SEP process, GitHub-hosted, not a standards body but functionally setting norms.
- **IETF** — 10+ agent-discovery drafts, all early, none adopted. Don't design against any of them.
- **OpenAI** — no standards push post-ai-plugin.json except their AGENTS.md adoption.
- **Google** — pushing A2A (Agent-to-Agent) as a sibling to MCP; not directly relevant to a wiki host.

**Directories to register in when launching.**
- https://llmstxthub.com/ — for llms.txt compliance (~900 sites)
- https://mcp.so/ and https://smithery.ai/ — MCP server directories (primary)
- https://mcpservers.org/ — claims 20k, lower-quality
- https://github.com/thedaviddias/llms-txt-hub — source-of-truth for llmstxthub
- https://agents.md/ registry — AGENTS.md adopters list

---

## Recommended minimum for ListHub's new wiki host (action list)

Ordered by leverage-per-hour. Every item maps to a section above.

1. **Content negotiation on every item URL.** `Accept: text/markdown` → raw markdown. Also `<url>.md` suffix fallback. `Vary: Accept` and `Link: rel=alternate`. *(§5)* This is the highest-leverage item on the list.
2. **`/llms.txt` + `/llms-full.txt`** auto-generated from the item store. Curated top-level, optional section for long tail. *(§2)*
3. **`/AGENTS.md` at site root** explaining signup, API, MCP, llms.txt. Prose, short, link-heavy. *(§4)*
4. **`/.well-known/mcp/server-card.json`** and **`/.well-known/mcp`**. Ship both SEP shapes; they're cheap and they future-proof you. *(§4)*
5. **Server-hosted MCP server** wrapping the existing REST API. Tools: `search`, `read_item`, `create_item`, `update_item`, `tag_item`, `list_items_by_user`. *(§1, §4)*
6. **Signup endpoint that returns an API key**, email optional, PoW fallback to captcha, `X-Agent-Name` soft header logged. *(§3)*
7. **WebMCP tool registration** on item edit pages: `read_page`, `edit_page`, `append_section`, `add_tag`. Ship behind feature-detect. *(§1)*
8. **Per-API-key scopes + per-key rate limits + `actor` column in audit log**. Even if unused today. *(§3, §6)*
9. **Delegation endpoint (`POST /api/v1/delegation`)** that mints short-lived scoped tokens from a PAT. Ship the shell now, the semantics can evolve. *(§6)*
10. **Register in llmstxthub, mcp.so, Smithery** at launch. *(§7)*

---

## What I verified and what I couldn't

Verified:
- All section-level claims are backed by live Q1 2026 search results (searches run 2026-04-08 in this session).
- Existing ListHub research (`llm-wiki-research.md`, `llm-wikis-catalog.md`) was checked and reused for llms.txt adopter lists and MCP-wiki peer list — not re-derived.
- URLs listed were all returned by WebSearch; I did not individually WebFetch each to confirm status codes.

Not verified / gaps:
- I did **not** confirm Chrome 146 WebMCP flag behavior on a real browser. The source is a Chrome developer blog post summary.
- SEP-1649/SEP-1960 exact field schemas — I have the shape, not line-by-line spec. Before implementing, read the GitHub discussion directly (link in §4).
- Cloudflare Markdown for Agents pricing/tier details — I have "Pro/Biz/Enterprise" from one source; verify on Cloudflare's pricing page before committing.
- Exact current count of llms.txt adopters — cited figures (900, 780, 13k) come from different directories with different inclusion criteria; none are authoritative.
- Anthropic's Feb 2026 OAuth policy — I have it from a Medium post and a GitHub issue, not from the Anthropic policy page itself. Confirm before citing externally.
- WebAuthn-for-agents is described as "consensus" — it's consensus *among security vendors and RSAC speakers*, not a formal W3C position.

If you want, next step is to WebFetch the top 3-5 spec sources directly (WebMCP draft, SEP-1649/1960 discussion, llms.txt spec, Cloudflare Markdown for Agents post) and pull exact schemas/field names into a second, implementation-ready doc.

### LMWiki Project Overview

- Four major epics identified for agent-first development
- Technical issues with repository causing display problems
  - Jump bug affecting interface
  - Espan functionality stopped working
- Timeline pressure identified - similar projects emerging rapidly
  - LMWiki references appeared 5 days ago
  - Window closing quickly - estimated 1 week maximum before market saturation
  - Need viral-ready minimal viable product

### Market Landscape Analysis

- 5-10 million estimated Obsidian users globally
- Quartz (Obsidian publisher) has 11k GitHub stars
  - Used by Stanford classes for course materials
  - Free alternative to Obsidian Publish ($20/month)
- Multiple competing projects launching simultaneously
  - Timeline from concept to market: ~3 weeks historically
  - Current competitive window much tighter

### Technical Architecture Discussion

- ListHub positioned as “cloud-based Obsidian”
- Core missing features identified:
  - Current search limited to individual pages
  - Need omnisearch with fuzzy search (Command+K binding)
  - Global vs local search options required
- FTP support needed for programmatic uploads
- Obsidian sync integration priority

### Development Strategy Options

- Three parallel development paths proposed:
  1. Build from scratch as backup
  2. Extend ListHub infrastructure
  3. Clone Quartz and add editing/permissions
- Agent-driven development approach
  - Deploy 1-3 agents on identified issues
  - Parallel processing to accelerate timeline
- Quartz integration advantages:
  - Leverage existing hard work
  - Folder index pages functionality
  - Established publishing framework

### Next Steps

- Add search functionality ticket creation
  - Fuzzy search over filenames and content
  - Global/local options
  - Command+K keybinding
- Evaluate Quartz modification feasibility
- Begin parallel development tracks immediately

content.text:
Name locked: **wikihub**. Research brief is in, transcripts parsed, and I'll stay in spec mode — no implementation, no more task creation without asking.

---

## Signal extracted from the transcripts

Filtering out shrimp, jaunty Jiffys, and Gemini debugging, here's the load-bearing stuff:

1. **Repo-per-user, not repo-per-wiki.** Direct quote: *"I like the idea of separate git repos for each user, because that's how people are naturally handling obsidian vaults. It's their own git repo or their agent memory. ... It's a repo per user."* This contradicts my v1 spec (repo-per-wiki). **Big fork** — I'll ask below.
2. **Unstructured-text → wiki is the viral hook, not a v2 nice-to-have.** The user story: *"I have a Google Doc / arbitrary scrap rambles / text conversation"* → wikihub. Paired with a broader **"omni-convert any file"** pattern (PDF, DOCX, .txt, even video transcripts): upload anything, we markdown-ize and commit.
3. **Cloud agent** — a shared headless Claude Code in print mode, sandboxed per-user via UNIX permissions on a per-user folder. *"Cortex might be overkill, just spin up a docker container, or even simpler: one process with per-user folder sandbox."* Explicitly: *"a cloud-based interface where you can just talk to an agent we host instead of one that you host ... especially nice on mobile."*
4. **Per-section privacy inside a page via HTML comments.** `<!-- private -->...<!-- /private -->` stripped out of the rendered public view but kept in the raw git blob. Micro-feature, but it's the bridge between per-file ACLs and the messy real-world case of a mostly-public wiki with a couple of spicy sentences.
5. **git-crypt escape hatch** for encrypted content on otherwise public wikis. Reference: jacobcole.net/labs. Probably not v1, but the architecture shouldn't preclude it (keep the raw blob flow transparent — don't re-encode markdown server-side).
6. **Omnisearch with Cmd+K.** Global + local, fuzzy across filenames and content. Listhub's current FTS is per-page; wikihub needs it cross-wiki from day one.
7. **Anthropic / Boris is the first-user target.** "They need a markdown reader; we want them using wikihub." This shapes v1: the reader view has to look *good* to a technical ML reader on day one. It also subtly argues for the repo-per-user model (that's how Boris would think about his own notes).
8. **Featured / community curation** on the homepage. /explore is not just "recent" — it's a curated slot.
9. **Migration targets queued up**: admitsphere, RSI wiki, systematic altruism wiki (currently on Google Sites), Jacob's CRM. We get 3-5 real wikis on wikihub in the first week without needing a marketing plan. This is the dogfood loop.
10. **Timeline is 1 week to viral-MVP.** Window-closing language is consistent across both transcripts. This hard-caps v1 scope — anything not on the critical path to "post on HN, Karpathy retweets" gets deferred.
11. **Three build options debated → from-scratch wins.** DokuWiki was floated and rejected (not markdown, PHP). Quartz-clone floated and deprioritized. Extend-listhub deprioritized. From-scratch is locked.
12. **Wikilink autocomplete** `[[...]]` in the editor is a listed gap.
13. **FTP** got mentioned as a desired upload path. Skeptical — git push covers it and FTP is a lot of surface for one user story. Flagging for veto.
14. **"Google Drive vibes"** — ACLs, per-file visibility, share-with-user, view/edit/comment/admin, link-sharing, unlisted. This is now a v1 requirement per the direct instruction. Full design below.

---

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- The repo-per-user decision is the single biggest fork in the spec and it ripples into **everything**: URL structure (`/@user/wiki` vs `/@user`), fork semantics (copy-a-folder vs clone-a-repo), ACL scoping (per-folder inside a shared repo is hostile to git, per-file is fine), omnisearch scoping (per-user is natural, cross-user is hard), and git-crypt compatibility (repo-per-user makes it trivial). The Obsidian-vault mental model is a strong signal it's the right call — but it forces fork to become a *logical* operation, not a git operation.
- The "1 week to viral MVP" constraint is real and it argues for a specific cut: ship the **reader + push + share + AGENTS.md + llms.txt + content negotiation** in week 1, defer the cloud agent, unstructured-text ingest, and rich social graph. The reader and the share model are the irreducible core — everything else is fancy icing that can ship in week 2-3 without losing the moment.
- The "Anthropic-partnership-via-Boris" framing is a secret forcing function: it means the reader UI for a *technical ML research wiki* has to look good on day one, but it also means you don't need fork/star/comments/PR-like flows for the pitch. Boris's wiki is solo. This gives you permission to defer most of the social graph.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

---

## The repo-per-user pivot — full implications

If we adopt it, the data model becomes:

```
User
  id, username, display_name, email?, noos_id?
  bare_repo_path = repos/<username>.git    # ONE repo, the user's whole vault

Wiki                  # now a logical grouping, not a physical repo
  id, owner_id → User
  slug              # e.g. "admit-sphere"
  root_path         # folder path inside owner's repo, e.g. "admit-sphere/"
  title, description
  visibility        # wiki-level default
  forked_from_id → Wiki?
  star_count

Page                  # one row per .md file in the user's repo
  id, owner_id → User
  wiki_id → Wiki?    # inferred by prefix-match against Wiki.root_path, nullable
  path              # e.g. "admit-sphere/entities/karpathy.md"
  title, frontmatter, content_excerpt
  visibility         # authoritative (overrides wiki default)
  updated_at
```

**URL structure:**
- `/@alice` — alice's vault landing (index.md at repo root, or a generated profile)
- `/@alice/admit-sphere` — a "wiki" view, which is just the subtree under `admit-sphere/` with its own `index.md`
- `/@alice/admit-sphere/entities/karpathy` — a specific page
- `git clone https://wikihub.example/@alice.git` — clones alice's entire vault (filtered server-side by visibility for the cloner's identity)

**Fork becomes a logical op, not a git op:**
- "Fork admit-sphere from alice" → wikihub copies the files under `admit-sphere/` into bob's repo as `admit-sphere/` (or a new slug if collision), commits with message `Forked from alice/admit-sphere@<sha>`, creates a `Wiki` row in bob's namespace with `forked_from_id` set. No separate bare repo. Git history starts fresh at the fork point but we record the provenance in Postgres.
- This is slightly less "pure" than git-native fork, but it matches the Obsidian vault model and it means a user with 10 wikis still has one repo to clone.

**Trade-offs vs repo-per-wiki:**

| | repo-per-user | repo-per-wiki |
|---|---|---|
| Matches Obsidian mental model | ✅ | ❌ |
| One `git clone` gets everything | ✅ | ❌ (one per wiki) |
| Fork is git-native | ❌ (logical copy) | ✅ |
| Wiki deletion | `git rm -r folder/` | delete bare repo |
| Per-user disk quota | trivial | annoying to sum |
| git-crypt | trivial (one keychain) | N keychains |
| Collab editing across wikis in your own vault | trivial | cross-repo dance |
| Cross-wiki wikilinks (`[[alice/admit-sphere/karpathy]]`) | natural | clumsy |
| Visibility enforcement on clone | server must filter (pack protocol) | trivial (own the repo or not) |
| Repo-size blowup risk | one user's wiki going viral bloats their vault | isolated |

**Filtering clones by visibility** is the one genuinely hard piece. Git Smart HTTP doesn't have per-blob auth. Options:
- **(a) Filter at pack time**: intercept `git-upload-pack`, walk the tree, exclude private blobs, rewrite refs. Custom and fragile.
- **(b) Per-visibility branches**: maintain `refs/heads/public` and `refs/heads/main` inside each user repo; `git clone` defaults to `public`; authenticated owner gets `main`. This is clean and Gitty and I like it a lot. Visibility writes update both branches with different trees. Post-receive runs on push to `main` and regenerates `public` by filtering.
- **(c) Clone is whole-repo, visibility enforced only in the web/API layer**. Simplest, but it leaks private blobs to anyone with a read token. Non-starter.

I'd recommend **(b) per-visibility branches** — it's the only answer that keeps `git clone` honest without custom wire protocol work.

---

## ACL / Google-Drive-style sharing (v1 requirement)

Per your direct instruction. Designing this as a first-class concern, not bolt-on.

**Permission levels** (ordered, higher includes lower):
- `none` — no access
- `read` — can see, clone, render
- `comment` — can add comments (v2 if we defer comments, but reserve the level)
- `edit` — can write pages
- `admin` — can change settings, change ACLs, delete

**Visibility modes** (per-wiki default, overridable per-file):
- `private` — only owner + explicit shares
- `restricted` — only explicit shares, but link-accessible (has a secret link)
- `unlisted` — public readable via direct URL but not indexed/searched, no directory listing
- `public` — indexed, searchable, appears in /explore
- `public_edit` — public + anyone with an account can edit (Wikipedia mode, applied per-page; logs `actor` for audit; protected against vandalism via revert + per-key rate limit)

**The ACL row:**

```
ShareGrant
  id
  target_type        # "wiki" | "page"
  target_id          # wiki_id or page_id
  grantee_type       # "user" | "email" | "public_link" | "anyone_signed_in"
  grantee_id         # user_id, email string, link_token, or null
  permission         # read | comment | edit | admin
  granted_by         # user_id
  granted_at
  expires_at         # nullable — link-shares can time out
```

**Resolution:**
- A read on page `P` in wiki `W` owned by `alice` requested by `bob`:
  1. If `bob == alice`, allow admin.
  2. Look up `ShareGrant` where `target_id = P.id`. If found, use that permission.
  3. Else look up `ShareGrant` where `target_id = W.id`. If found, use that.
  4. Else use `W.visibility` (the wiki-level default).
  5. Most permissive wins if multiple grants apply.
- **Page ACL overrides wiki ACL.** A page can be more *or* less permissive than its wiki — a public wiki with one private page, or a private wiki with one publicly-shared page, both work.

**The frontmatter hint:**
```yaml
---
title: Karpathy
visibility: public       # hint; authoritative value lives in Postgres
share:
  - { user: bob, permission: edit }
  - { link: true, permission: read }
---
```
Frontmatter is just a hint written by the agent or user. On push, post-receive reads it and reconciles with Postgres: if the hint differs from the stored ACL, Postgres wins *unless* the pusher has `admin` on the resource, in which case the hint is applied. This keeps the "agent writes YAML, it just works" flow while preventing a compromised git push from escalating privileges.

**Per-section private blocks** (from transcript point #4):
```markdown
# Karpathy

Public overview here.

<!-- private -->
Secret note only owner sees. Gets stripped from public render and from llms.txt / .md responses, but stays in the git blob.
<!-- /private -->

More public content.
```
The markdown parser strips `<!-- private -->...<!-- /private -->` blocks when rendering for a viewer who doesn't have `read` on the parent page's *private bands*. Implementation: a single boolean per viewer — "can see private bands on this page?" — computed from the ACL. Ship in v1; it's ~30 lines of code and it unlocks the "mostly public with spicy bits" use case that the transcript spent real time on.

**What we are NOT doing in v1:** real-time collaborative editing (CRDTs, OT, presence, cursors). The architecture reserves space for it — each page edit goes through the API, which can later become an op-log feed — but v1 is last-write-wins with git conflict resolution. *("We're going to want some kind of good collaborative editing, but we don't need to bias towards real-time immediately yet"* — direct quote, locked in.)

---

## Research-brief integration — "Agent surfaces" section of the spec

This is a new section of the spec, fully integrated from the research brief. This is the highest-leverage chunk of v1 work per the brief's own ranking.

### A1. Content negotiation (*highest leverage, §5 of brief*)

Every page URL responds to three things:
- `GET /@user/wiki/page` with `Accept: text/html` → Jinja-rendered dark-theme view
- `GET /@user/wiki/page` with `Accept: text/markdown` → raw markdown with frontmatter, no chrome
- `GET /@user/wiki/page.md` → same as the markdown negotiation path (fallback for dumb clients)

Response headers:
- `Vary: Accept` (cache correctness)
- `Link: </@user/wiki/page.md>; rel="alternate"; type="text/markdown"`
- HTML responses include JSON-LD `@type: Article` with author/dates/license

**Private bands are stripped from both html and markdown responses** based on the requesting identity. The raw blob on `git clone` is a *separate* code path (per-visibility branches, see above).

Claude Code and OpenCode already send `Accept: text/markdown` on every fetch (Vercel confirmed Feb 2026). This one change makes wikihub's entire content corpus immediately usable by every coding agent in the ecosystem *without any client-side config*. It's the single biggest agent-compatibility move in the spec.

### A2. llms.txt + llms-full.txt (*§2 of brief*)

Auto-generated per-user and per-wiki:
- `/@alice/llms.txt` — curated index of alice's public pages, grouped by wiki, with an `## Optional` section for low-priority pages (Stripe pattern)
- `/@alice/llms-full.txt` — every public page concatenated, Vercel-style
- `/@alice/admit-sphere/llms.txt` — same but scoped to one wiki
- `/llms.txt` at site root — curated list of *featured* wikis across users

Generation rule: on every push to a user's repo, regenerate their `llms.txt` and `llms-full.txt` files as derived artifacts, served from Postgres (not committed to the repo — don't pollute history).

Brief is explicit that llms.txt is **table stakes, not a retrieval signal**. We ship it for credibility, not distribution. The real distribution channel is A1 (content negotiation).

### A3. Capability manifests (*§4 of brief*)

Ship all four cheap ones:

- **`/AGENTS.md`** at the site root. Prose, short, link-heavy. "You are a coding agent. Here's how to sign up on wikihub. Here's your API base URL. Here's the MCP server. Here's llms.txt. Here's the rate limit policy. Here's how to respect robots.txt and private pages." This is the *only* format with real multi-vendor adoption (OpenAI Codex, Claude Code, Cursor) — per the brief it is specifically the thing that has landed.
- **`/.well-known/mcp/server-card.json`** (SEP-1649 shape) — rich metadata, tools list, auth, homepage.
- **`/.well-known/mcp`** (SEP-1960 shape) — endpoint enumeration + auth discovery. Brief says major clients implement both speculatively → we ship both.
- **`/openapi.json`** — the existing REST API in OpenAPI format, linked from llms.txt.
- **`/.well-known/wikihub.json`** — site-specific bootstrap manifest for agents that speaks our own shape (API base, MCP URL, signup URL, docs URL). This is what lets an agent pointed at just `wikihub.example` self-bootstrap without reading human docs.

### A4. Server-hosted MCP (*§1 of brief*)

Standalone MCP server shipped as `wikihub-mcp` (distributable as pip + npm). Tools:
- `whoami`
- `search(query, scope?)` — fuzzy search, scope = "mine" | "public" | "all-accessible"
- `read_page(path)`
- `list_pages(wiki?, prefix?)`
- `create_page(path, content, frontmatter?)`
- `update_page(path, content, frontmatter?)`
- `append_section(path, heading, content)`
- `delete_page(path)`
- `set_visibility(target, visibility)` — target can be wiki or page
- `share(target, grantee, permission)`
- `create_wiki(slug, title, description?)`
- `fork_wiki(source_owner, source_slug, new_slug?)`
- `commit_log(wiki, limit?)`

Thin wrapper over the REST API. Authenticates via Bearer API key. Every tool call logs `X-Agent-Name` if sent.

### A5. WebMCP (*§1 of brief*)

In-browser tool registration on wiki view/edit pages using `navigator.modelContext.registerTool(...)`. Feature-detected (Chrome 146+ early preview only as of the brief). Tools registered on each page:
- `read_current_page`
- `edit_current_page(markdown)`
- `append_section_to_current_page(heading, markdown)`
- `add_tag_to_current_page(tag)`
- `link_current_page_to(other_path)`

Reuses the logged-in browser session — no API key needed for in-tab agents. Gated behind the spec-required human confirmation step. v1-lite: ship the registration code behind a feature flag so it's there but off by default until we've tested it on a real Chrome 146 build.

### A6. Agent-native auth (*§3 of brief, already in spec — reinforced*)

- Already-specced signup returning API key with optional email stays as-is.
- Add **per-key scopes** from day one (`read`, `write`, `admin`), even if the default key is `read+write`. Brief says you'll want them later; schema-migrating scopes after the fact is hell.
- Add **per-key rate limits** separate from per-user.
- Add **`actor` column** in the audit log (null today, ready for RFC 8693 delegation tokens later).
- Log `X-Agent-Name` / `X-Agent-Version` headers on every API call; surface in the user's dashboard ("this key was used by `claude-code@1.2.3`"). Users love this.
- 429 responses return JSON `{error, retry_after_seconds, quota_remaining}` not HTML.
- Read GETs of `.md` content are exempt from aggressive limits. Encourage scraping — it's the point.
- **Signup rate limit**: 5/hour per IP. PoW token as escape hatch for headless signups that hit the IP cap. No CAPTCHA on API calls ever.

### A7. Launch registrations (*§7 of brief*)

On launch day, submit to: llmstxthub, mcp.so, Smithery, mcpservers.org, the agents.md registry, deepwiki.directory, and thedaviddias/llms-txt-hub PR. Cross-link from the existing listhub `agent-friendly-apps` list as a self-reference.

---

## Consolidated spec delta from this round

Additions/changes since the last spec draft, in order of importance:

1. **Name: wikihub.**
2. **Repo-per-user architecture** (pending your confirmation on the fork).
3. **Per-visibility git branches** (`refs/heads/public` as a filtered view of `refs/heads/main`) to keep `git clone` honest.
4. **Full ACL system** with permission levels (read/comment/edit/admin), visibility modes (private/restricted/unlisted/public/public_edit), `ShareGrant` table, frontmatter hints reconciled against DB on push, page ACLs overriding wiki ACLs.
5. **Private bands** (`<!-- private -->` HTML comments) in v1.
6. **Agent surfaces section** (A1–A7) folded in from the research brief.
7. **Unstructured-text → wiki** promoted to v1.5 (ship within 2 weeks of v1, before the "1 week window" fully closes) — it's the viral hook, not a v2.
8. **Cloud agent** (headless Claude Code, per-user folder sandbox via UNIX perms) promoted to v1.5.
9. **Omni-convert upload** (any file → markdown → commit) promoted to v1.5.
10. **Cmd+K omnisearch** — global + local, fuzzy, in v1 (ships with the reader).
11. **`[[wikilink]]` autocomplete in the editor** — v1.
12. **Dogfood migrations** (admitsphere, RSI wiki, systematic altruism, Jacob's CRM) listed as explicit v1-week-2 tasks.
13. **Anthropic/Boris reader-quality target** — the HTML reader view is held to a higher bar than I originally scoped.

---
